query: When is the premiere of 'Carole King & James Taylor: Just Call Out My Name'?
positive: So, guess what? There was a concert tour to celebrate their 40th anniversary! Carole King and James Taylor might have a few more years under their belts since they first hit the stage together, but let me tell you, they haven’t missed a beat! The concert film features some fantastic songs, including: (You Make Me Feel Like) A Natural Woman, Something in the Way She Moves, So Far Away, Carolina in My Mind, Country Road, Smackwater Jack, and Where You Lead (they even switched up the lyrics to fit the city they were performing in instead of New York), Your Smiling Face, Beautiful, Shower The People, Way Over Yonder, Sweet Baby James (which kicks off the second half of the film), Up on the Roof, It’s Too Late, Fire and Rain, I Feel the Earth Move, You’ve Got a Friend, How Sweet It Is (To Be Loved by You), and Mexico for the end credits. The film was directed by Frank Marshall and features an amazing lineup with Carole King, James Taylor, Danny Kortchmar, Peter Asher, Russ Kunkel, and Leland Sklar, plus some additional musicians like Andrea Zonn, Arnold McCuller, Kate Markowitz, and Robbie Kondor. If you’re interested, Carole King & James Taylor: Just Call Out My Name premiered on January 2, 2022, at 9:00 PM ET/PT on CNN. You can catch the film on demand through cable/satellite systems, CNNgo platforms, and CNN mobile apps starting January 3 through January 16.

You'll also hear "Country Road," "Smackwater Jack," and a special version of "Where You Lead" that changes the lyrics to fit the city they're performing in. Other songs include "Your Smiling Face," "Beautiful," "Shower The People," "Way Over Yonder," and "Sweet Baby James," which kicks off the second half of the film! Don't miss classics like "Up on the Roof," "It’s Too Late," "Fire and Rain," "I Feel the Earth Move," "You’ve Got a Friend," and "How Sweet It Is (To Be Loved by You)." The film wraps up with "You Can Close Your Eyes" and "Mexico" during the end credits. 

It’s directed by Frank Marshall and features talents like Carole King, James Taylor, Danny Kortchmar, and more. And if you want to catch it, "Carole King & James Taylor: Just Call Out My Name" premiered on January 2, 2022, at 9:00pm ET/PT on CNN. Plus, you can watch it on demand starting January 3 through January 16 on your cable/satellite systems, CNNgo platforms, and CNN mobile apps. It's all about this amazing musical partnership that Rolling Stone magazine – and only them, it seems – probably ranks super high on their list of the greatest things ever. So, here’s the scoop: back in 1970, Taylor and King kicked off their first concert together at the iconic Troubadour club in Hollywood. And that was just the start of a beautiful friendship – totally platonic, I promise! They made some incredible music together, and I know it sounds cheesy, but it’s true. They sang each other’s songs, lifted each other up, and harmonized like tea and honey. Fast forward forty years, and they decided to celebrate their connection with a tour of over 50 dates. Now, 12 years after that, we get to enjoy a celebration of that celebration – a throwback to all the joy they brought to so many people 52 years ago, then again 12 years ago, and now, with this movie, a third time around. Just Call Out My Name kicks off with clips of late-night hosts like Leno, Carson, and Letterman introducing Taylor or King or both, and then there’s a little moment with Oprah giving Taylor a shout-out. Now, “Just Call Out My Name” might seem like it’s a bit too polished, almost like it’s trying to sell something, but really, it’s just a celebration of these incredible songs, preserved in digital form from 2010 for us to enjoy in 2022. But honestly, the feelings they shared on stage back in 1970 and again 40 years later were so genuine, and their songs never feel like they were churned out for the masses. query: Who won the 2022 Citrus Bowl?
positive:
The 2022 Citrus Bowl was a college football game that took place on January 1, 2022, kicking off at 1:00 p.m. EST and aired on ABC. This marked the 76th time the Citrus Bowl was held, and it was part of the 2021–22 bowl games wrapping up the 2021 FBS football season. The game was officially called the VRBO Citrus Bowl, thanks to sponsorship from Vrbo, which is a vacation rental marketplace owned by Expedia. 

In this matchup, we had the Kentucky Wildcats from the Southeastern Conference (SEC) going up against the Iowa Hawkeyes from the Big Ten Conference. It was the first time these two teams faced each other. Both teams got their invitations on Sunday, December 5. Kentucky came into the game with a solid 9–3 record (5–3 in the SEC) and held the No. 22 spot in the final CFP poll. This was their second time playing in the Citrus Bowl – they had previously been there in 2019. On the other hand, the Hawkeyes were the Big Ten West Division champs with a 10–3 record (7–2 in the B1G) and were ranked No. 15 in the final CFP poll, making their second Citrus Bowl appearance as well, the last one being in 2005. The game took place at Camping World Stadium in Orlando, Florida.

So, here’s a quick recap of the Iowa Hawkeyes versus Kentucky Wildcats college football game that went down on January 1, 2022. The final score was 17-20, and you can catch all the highlights on ESPN!
negative: "Quad Wilson snagged an interception from Jack Albers and took it all the way back for a 99-yard touchdown! That play really capped off the 2022-23 season perfectly, leading to a big Citrus Bowl win with a score of 63-7. What a way to finish!"

Hey there! So, here’s the scoop from Orlando: LSU’s offense really turned up the heat, racking up an impressive 594 yards to crush Purdue 63-7, snagging the Cheez-It Citrus Bowl title at Camping World Stadium on Monday. With this win, LSU wraps up the season with a 10-4 record. 

Now, let’s talk about some standout performances. Quarterback Jayden Daniels had a solid game, completing 12 of 17 passes for 139 yards and a touchdown. Meanwhile, redshirt freshman Garrett Nussmeier did his part too, going 10 of 14 for 98 yards, tossing a touchdown, but also had an interception. And how about sophomore wide receiver Malik Nabers? He was named Citrus Bowl MVP, pulling in nine catches for 163 yards and a touchdown. Freshman tight end Mason Taylor also made his mark with five catches for 88 yards and a touchdown. On the defensive side, senior safety Greg Brooks was all over the field, notching five tackles (one for a loss) and grabbing an interception.

As for the key plays, Purdue won the toss but decided to defer until the second half. On LSU's first drive, they were at the 44-yard line when Daniels connected with Taylor for a nice 19-yard gain to the Purdue 44. Then, on a crucial 4th-and-2 from the 36, the Tigers went for it, and Daniels hit Nabers for a 12-yard gain that kept the drive alive at the Boilermaker 24. Right after that, Daniels found Nabers again for a 9-yard pickup down to the Purdue 15. Exciting stuff, right?

Hey there! So, college football's bowl season is in full swing, and that week between Christmas and New Year's just feels like the perfect time to shout out the players who really shined this postseason and, of course, mention those who didn't quite measure up. Let’s dive into the winners and losers of the 2022-23 bowl season — at least for now. We’ll keep this updated as bowl season rolls on through January 2.

First up, LSU: The Tigers wrapped up Brian Kelly’s first season with a bang, hitting that 10-win mark with a whopping 63-7 victory over Purdue on January 2. They were totally dominating, leading 35-0 by halftime and 49-0 before Purdue finally managed to score. The Boilermakers were missing key players like QB Aidan O’Connell and WR Charlie Jones, but honestly, this was a bigger blowout than anyone saw coming. LSU even got to play three quarterbacks, and WR Malik Nabers got in on the action too, throwing two passes — one of which ended up being a touchdown — along with racking up nine catches for 163 yards and another TD. They came just six yards shy of hitting the 600-yard mark on offense, plus they snagged a 99-yard pick six right before the clock ran out. It was just a total blowout.

Now, let’s talk about Oregon State: They completely crushed Florida 30-3 in the Las Vegas Bowl, marking their first 10-win season since 2006! And guess what? It was also their first bowl win since 2013. How awesome is that?

In their latest win, Oregon State really put the brakes on Florida, holding them to just 219 yards of offense. The Gators only managed to put points on the board with a field goal in the last 37 seconds of the game. Before bouncing back to a 7-6 record in 2021, Oregon State had a rough patch with seven straight losing seasons. But now, the Beavers have turned things around, going 17-9 in the last two years. Plus, they’re bringing in Clemson transfer quarterback DJ Uiagalelei next season, which could make them a popular pick to challenge for the Pac-12 title. 

As for Florida State, they’re celebrating their first 10-win season since 2016 after a nail-biting 35-32 victory over Oklahoma in the Cheez-It Bowl. Jordan Travis was on fire, throwing for 418 yards on 38 attempts, while Johnny Wilson racked up eight catches for 202 yards. The Seminoles put together a whopping 587 yards on 72 plays and still managed to win despite struggling on fourth down, going 0-of-3. This is their first bowl win since 2017, which is a huge confidence boost as they head into 2023, looking to be the main contender against Clemson in the ACC.

And let’s not forget about Pitt’s Nick Patti. He’s been a backup for five seasons, but he finally got the chance to start in the Sun Bowl against UCLA, and wow, did he make it count! In just his third career start, Patti led the Panthers to an incredible comeback win.

Did you know that the Cheez-It Citrus Bowl is the seventh-oldest collegiate bowl game in the U.S.? It actually started back in 1947 as the Tangerine Bowl! The first game was funded by some members of Elks Lodge #1079 in Orlando, who each chipped in $100 to cover the initial costs. Since 1993, it’s been the go-to spot for top teams from the Big Ten and Southeastern conferences. Even with the new College Football Playoff era shaking things up, the Cheez-It Citrus Bowl is still set to feature the best teams from those conferences that aren’t part of the CFP series, like Rose, Sugar, Fiesta, Orange, Cotton, and Peach bowls. 

Oh, and since 1987, this game has been a New Year’s Day tradition—except for those rare years when the holiday lands on a Sunday. If you’re gearing up for game day, you can find all the info you need, like parking, stadium policies, maps, and schedules. And if you’re planning a trip to Orlando, we’ve got a guide to help you with where to stay and what to do. Plus, stay in the loop with all the latest news about the Cheez-It Citrus Bowl, including event details and presale opportunities. Florida Citrus Sports is all about boosting community spirit and pride, promoting tourism, and supporting local charities and educational institutions through its events.

Hey there! So, here’s the scoop on the players who won’t be hitting the field on Monday in Orlando—LSU is really feeling the pinch. The Purdue Boilermakers are set to take on the LSU Tigers in the 2023 Citrus Bowl at Camping World Stadium, and kickoff is at 1:00 p.m. ET on ABC. It looks like it could be an exciting matchup, but honestly, it’s not quite what we hoped for since so many players are sitting this one out—more than any other bowl game this season. The big names missing include Aidan O’Connell from Purdue and Jayden Daniels from LSU, both of whom have been starting quarterbacks all season long. O’Connell had an impressive 3,490 yards last year in his super-senior season. Daniels has announced he’ll be back in Baton Rouge next year, but unfortunately, he’s injured and won’t be able to play in this game. Here’s the latest rundown of the players from both teams who won’t be participating in the 2022 Citrus Bowl.

Wow, the 2022 Vrbo Citrus Bowl was such an exciting game, full of those crazy momentum shifts and nail-biting drives from both teams! But for the Hawkeyes and their fans, it’s a tough pill to swallow as they head back to Iowa after a 20-17 loss to Mark Stoops and the SEC's Kentucky. They really had a shot at this one, starting with the ball and a timeout at their own 25, just down by three with 1:48 on the clock. Things looked promising when they made it to Kentucky's 40 with only 48 seconds left. But then, Spencer Petras threw a pass that went way off target, landing right in DeAndre Square’s hands at the Wildcats' 26. And just like that, it was game over. Kentucky made an impressive drive, with Wan'Dale Robinson pulling off a fantastic 52-yard catch-and-run to bring his total to 17 catches for 170 yards that day. Chris Rodriguez Jr. capped off that eight-play, 80-yard drive with a nice 6-yard touchdown run. So, could Iowa’s offense, which really stepped up in the second half compared to the first, pull off a win for their defense? Oh, and by the way, Tyler Linderbaum was back in the game. Unfortunately, Iowa went three-and-out. They tried to draw Kentucky offside on a fourth-and-1, but it didn’t work out, so Tory Taylor ended up punting.

The Aggies kicked off their season pretty rocky at 1-5, but they really turned things around by winning six out of their last seven games, finishing with a 7-6 record. It's impressive, especially since they only managed to win eight games over the past four seasons. Coach Kill has done an amazing job in his first year! Diego Pavia really shined in the bowl win, throwing for 167 yards and two touchdowns, plus he added 65 rushing yards. He made some crucial third-down conversions with his legs in the second half, which helped seal the victory for his team. 

Now, let's talk about Southern Miss and their running back Frank Gore Jr. He absolutely dominated in their win over Rice in the LendingTree Bowl, setting a bowl record with 329 yards on just 21 carries in a 38-24 victory. He rushed for two touchdowns and even caught an 18-yard touchdown pass. The previous record was 317 yards, but Gore broke that with a stunning 55-yard touchdown run in the closing minutes. Southern Miss only won six games combined in 2020 and 2021, but this bowl win brought them to seven wins for the 2022 season. With Gore and a lot of key players expected to come back, Southern Miss might be a team to watch in the Sun Belt next season. And don’t forget Eastern Michigan—they clinched their first bowl victory since 1987 with a win over San Jose State in the Potato Bowl!

You know, Western Kentucky really put on a show during bowl season! They took down South Alabama in the New Orleans Bowl with a solid 44-23 win, and honestly, it wasn’t even that close. South Alabama was hoping for their first-ever bowl win, but WKU came out strong, leading 31-3 at halftime and just kept the momentum going, racking up an impressive 677 total yards by the end. Austin Reed was on fire, throwing for 497 yards and four touchdowns!

Then we have Air Force, who seemed perfectly at ease despite the freezing temperatures in Fort Worth. They steamrolled Baylor with a 30-15 victory in the Armed Forces Bowl, rushing for 276 yards while holding Baylor to just 42 rushing yards. With this win, Air Force hit double-digit victories for the third time in four seasons—pretty impressive! They went 11-2 in 2019 and 10-3 in the last two seasons, though the 2020 season was a bit of a downer at 3-3. But hey, they’ve also won four bowl games in a row now!

And let’s not forget about New Mexico State, which wrapped up Year 1 of the Jerry Kill era with a bowl win. They edged out Bowling Green 24-19 in the Quick Lane Bowl on Monday, marking just their second bowl victory since 1960!

Wow, what a game! If you’re not a fan of punting, this one was definitely for you. But for Ole Miss QB Jaxson Dart, it was a tough outing—he ended up throwing three picks and fumbled once. On the flip side, Texas Tech QB Tyler Shough had quite the day, rushing for two touchdowns and throwing for another. Unfortunately for Ole Miss, that loss means they wrap up the season at 8-5 after starting off strong at 7-0.

Still, that puts them at a 6-7 finish for the season, marking the first losing record for OU since 1998. It’s been a bit of a bumpy ride in the Brent Venables era, especially when you look at the success Caleb Williams and Lincoln Riley found at USC. But no need to hit the panic button in Norman… not just yet.

As for SMU, they kicked off the New Mexico Bowl as 4.5-point favorites against BYU, who had a freshman third-string QB, Sol-Jay Maiava-Peters, starting for the first time. You won’t believe how Iowa’s quarterbacks struggled this season—they only tossed seven touchdowns in total, while the defense managed to score six times! Now, let’s talk about UCLA. Sure, UCLA managed to score with just 34 seconds left to go ahead 35-34, and it looked like they might have it in the bag, but their defense let Pitt march down the field in just 30 seconds to kick the game-winning field goal. Without their offensive coordinator Will Stein—who's now at Oregon—the usually sharp UTSA offense had a rough time, turning the ball over five times. They also racked up nine penalties for 104 yards, and many of those flags came at really bad moments. A win against the Trojans would have been a huge boost for UTSA as they gear up to join the AAC in 2023. As for NC State, it seems like head coach Dave Doeren's cautious play calling might have cost them a bowl victory. They fell to Maryland in the Duke's Mayo Bowl, 16-12. And then, when they were trailing 13-9 in the fourth quarter, he sent out the kicker again instead of trying to go for it on fourth-and-3 from the 9.

Wow, it’s been a long time coming for Eastern Michigan! Before Chris Creighton took over as head coach in 2014, the last time the program went to a bowl game was back in 1987 for the California Bowl. Just like the earlier Iowa and Minnesota matchup that had a pregame total of 31.5 and ended up being a 13-10 game, this one went under too. The Aztecs’ last turnover happened with just 55 seconds left on a 4th and 24 when Tyrell Shavers fumbled after a 12-yard gain.

He threw for 224 yards and added another 73 on the ground, including a tough 11-yard scramble that set up the field goal that won the game. This one was a 47-yarder and clinched the win. Pitt coach Pat Narduzzi has already said that Patti won’t be returning next season, so if that was his last college game, he definitely made it memorable. 

Now, let’s talk about Fresno State. After a rough 1-4 start, they turned things around and finished the season with nine straight wins. They beat Boise State in the Mountain West title game, then cruised past Washington State 29-6 in the LA Bowl, marking their fourth season with 10 wins in the last six years. They even made history as the first team in FBS to reach 10 wins after starting the season with four losses in five games. The LA Bowl was also Jake Haener's last college game, and he certainly went out with a bang, throwing for 280 yards and two touchdowns, while Jordan Mims rushed for 209 yards and scored twice himself.

Remember, the team struggled a lot in David Cutcliffe’s last years, finishing a rough 5-18 (1-17 in the ACC). But with Wednesday’s win, Duke ended the season at 9-4 overall and 5-3 in ACC play. Now, Semore is back at Georgia Tech for the second time in his career after spending a year at Valdosta State. He’s stepping in to take over the linebackers from Defensive Coordinator Andrew Thacker.

So, let’s take a look at Semore’s coaching journey. He started off as the Defensive Coordinator at Round Valley High School in Arizona back in 2006. Then, in 2007, he moved to the Colorado School of Mines as a Secondary Coach. From 2008 to 2009, he was at Adams State, juggling roles as Co-Defensive Coordinator, Secondary Coach, and Special Teams Coordinator. He spent 2010 to 2011 coaching Outside Linebackers at Tulsa, and then from 2012 to 2013, he worked as a Defensive Assistant at Oklahoma State. After that, he returned to the Colorado School of Mines as the Defensive Coordinator in 2014. In 2015, he was at Montana as the Secondary Coach and Special Teams Coordinator, and then from 2016 to 2017, he took on the role of Defensive Coordinator there too. In 2018, he moved to Temple as the Senior Defensive Advisor, and for the last couple of years, 2019 to 2020, he served as a Defensive Analyst at Georgia Tech. Most recently, in 2021, he’s been the Defensive Coordinator at Valdosta State.

Honestly, Semore has more experience than I’d realized, but this role is definitely the biggest challenge he’s faced so far. At Valdosta State, his defense really shone, ranking in the top 20 nationally in defensive touchdowns, pass defense, redzone defense, and fumble recoveries. They even made it to the national championship game, though they didn’t quite take home the trophy. Now, while these two coaches bring different levels of experience to the table, I really think they’re stepping in for Andrew Thacker, who might still hold the title of defensive coordinator but isn’t coaching the linebackers anymore.

“So, he really got the whole defense and how it all worked, you know? I thought, ‘Wow, this young guy is totally ready for this.’” When Stitt became the head coach at FCS Montana back in December 2014, he brought Semore on board first as the safeties coach and special-teams coordinator, then bumped him up to defensive coordinator. “He connects really well with the players, and he just knows how to get the best out of them,” Stitt said. “They really give it their all for him.” Oh, and it was at Oklahoma State where Gundy gave him the nickname Boogie. Funny enough, it had nothing to do with Semore himself; it was actually the name of an assistant who had just left the team. “I think it was probably because he didn’t remember my name,” Semore chuckled. “It was right when I got hired, and then everyone just started calling me that.” His time at Oklahoma State also linked him up with Andrew Thacker, who was a GA back then. Thacker helped bring him to Temple in 2018 as a defensive analyst, and then Collins took him to Tech for a similar role before he got the defensive coordinator position at Division II Valdosta State. And about that nickname—Malone, the Kansas State assistant coach who brought him to Oklahoma State from Tulsa, said, “I remember Jason always being like, ‘Man, he doesn’t even know my name’ when he first got there.”

So, Semore has been with Tech for the last couple of seasons, but before that, he spent a season at Temple and three at Montana, where he was the special teams coordinator and secondary coach in 2015, then moved up to defensive coordinator for two years in 2016 and 2017. With Semore leading the defense and a lot of experienced players back, Goff is really looking forward to seeing the 2021 version of the Black Swarm being just as tough and aggressive as before. And hey, Valdosta State has been known as the Black Swarm since my playing days, and we’re going to keep that name alive and meet those expectations." Hey there, it’s Andy Demetra, the voice of the Yellow Jackets! Let’s dive into something interesting about our new special teams coordinator, Jason Semore. You know the story: a kid grows up as the son of a small-town coach, right? He follows in his dad's footsteps, works hard, and climbs the coaching ladder. Sounds familiar, doesn’t it? Well, that’s where things get a bit different for Jason. 

He played for his dad in high school and went on to letter in college, but then he spent 17 years bouncing around from Division II to FCS to FBS, coast to coast, moving from small-school coordinator to Power-5 analyst, and now he’s finally landed at Georgia Tech as a linebackers coach. Just recently, he got promoted to special teams coordinator for the Yellow Jackets, a role he’s held before back in 2015 at the University of Montana. 

But here’s the kicker: Jason’s background sets him apart from most coach’s kids at the Division I level—maybe even all of them. And it all boils down to something simple at the top of his bio: Hometown: Ganado, Ariz. It’s easy to overlook or just skim through that info, but it tells a deeper story about who he is. Every coach and player has their hometown listed, but Jason’s is a bit more special.

Jason Semore is a well-known name in the sports scene of Ganado, Arizona. These days, he’s sharing his football expertise as a linebackers coach at Georgia Tech University in Atlanta. It’s a big-time research school and part of the Atlantic Coast Conference. Although he still has some strength in his throwing arm and his legs are in good shape, that’s not what’s on his mind right now. “I played football, baseball, track and field, and I even wrestled at Ganado High School,” says Semore, who’s now 40 and was part of the Hornets’ football teams that racked up championships back in the late '90s and early 2000s. “Baseball was my first love, but I was always around football because my dad was a coach.” Jason was born in Portland, Oregon, but he spent most of his childhood on the Navajo Nation in Ganado. His grandparents lived there in the '70s, and he called the reservation home for about 17 years. He played linebacker and running back in football, was a catcher and third baseman in baseball, and sprinted in track. When it comes to football, he always leaned towards defense and really enjoys coaching that side of the game.

"You know, I've always leaned towards defense, both when I played and now as a coach," Semore shared. "Defense feels more reactive to me, and I loved playing with emotion—there was just less thinking involved. Offense, on the other hand, is all about execution and assignments. Sure, defense has those elements too, but it’s really driven by effort and technique." He mentioned that his dad, Russ, who coached him at Ganado High, was a big inspiration for him to get into coaching. Russ had gone to Ganado High himself, then came back after college and the military to kick off his coaching career. After finishing up at Ganado, Jason went on to play college football at Adams State University in Colorado. His coaching journey has taken him to several places, including Adams State, the Colorado School of Mines, the University of Tulsa, Oklahoma State University, the University of Montana, Temple University, Valdosta State University, and Georgia Tech. Although he wasn't drafted into the NFL after college, he looked up to linebackers like Dan Morgan from the Carolina Panthers and Hall of Famer Brian Urlacher, who's from New Mexico and played for the Chicago Bears, as models for his playing style. "My dad was my biggest inspiration," Jason said. "He really taught me about the importance of being part of a team and something bigger than myself. Since he was always coaching, I got to see how investing in others and team dynamics can bring joy to life."

“I went into the game feeling totally ready, but I’d be lying if I said I wasn’t downing Pepto Bismol in the coaching box because of an ulcer,” Semore joked. “And let me tell you, the guys really stepped up their game.” He was also part of the decision to promote kicker Gavin Stewart, who nailed all four of his field goals against Pitt after Jude Kelley had a rough start in the first four games. “Coach Key always says it’s crazy to keep doing the same things and expect different results,” Semore explained. “So, we all agreed it was time to switch things up and give some new guys a shot.” One of those new guys was Semore himself, who has really made the most of his opportunity. By the way, Ken Sugiura is a sports columnist at the Atlanta Journal-Constitution. He’s been with the AJC since 1998, covering all sorts of sports stories.

"Hey everyone! We’re almost done introducing Tech’s new defensive coaches. Today’s the second day where we’ll dive into two of them. First up, we have David Turner, who’s the Assistant Head Coach for defense and also the Defensive Run Game Coordinator. I know, that title’s a bit of a mouthful! But honestly, I think he might just be the best hire Tech has made this time around. He’ll be teaming up with Brent Key as another Assistant Head Coach and will be working alongside DB Coach Travares Tillman, who’s in charge of the Defensive Pass Game. Let’s get into it!"

So, let’s take a look at Turner’s coaching journey. He kicked things off in 1986 at Davidson, where he was the Running Backs and Tight Ends Coach. Then, in 1987, he switched it up to Linebackers Coach at the same school. From 1988 to 1989, he was a Graduate Assistant at NC State. In 1990, he took on the role of Defensive Line Coach at Indiana University of Pennsylvania. After that, he spent 1991 and 1992 as an Assistant Coach at James Madison. Then, from 1993 to 1994, he was the Defensive Ends Coach at Kentucky. 

He returned to NC State from 1995 to 1996 to coach Defensive Tackles and Linebackers. From 1997 to 2000, he moved to Virginia as the Defensive Tackles and Linebackers Coach. In 2001, he was the Defensive Ends Coach at Minnesota. Turner then joined Vanderbilt from 2002 to 2005 as their Defensive Line Coach and Recruiting Coordinator. In 2006, he was back to being the Defensive Ends Coach at Alabama. 

After that, he was at Mississippi State as the Defensive Line Coach from 2007 to 2009, followed by another stint at Kentucky from 2010 to 2012 as the Defensive Line Coach. He returned to Mississippi State again from 2013 to 2015 for the same role. From 2016 to 2017, he was at Texas A&M, where he served as the Run Defense Coordinator and Defensive Tackles Coach. He then took the Defensive Line Coach position at Texas-San Antonio in 2018 and wrapped up that phase of his career at Florida as the Defensive Line Coach from 2019 to 2021. So, when Turner joins the team, he’s not just any coach; he comes in with a wealth of experience that really stands out on the staff!

You know, even though Semore had a dad who coached, he’s aware that his upbringing—growing up on a reservation and playing Division II football—didn’t exactly give him the same connections that many other aspiring college coaches have. But he says his time in Ganado, surrounded by the Navajo culture, really taught him how to feel comfortable in different environments. That skill has been a game-changer for him in coaching. “Building relationships just came naturally to me, and it really mattered to me, which has helped me get to where I am today,” Semore shared. query: Which company acquired ShowBiz Cinemas?
positive: Evo Entertainment Group just made a big move by buying 100 percent of Showbiz Cinemas in an all-cash deal. They haven’t shared all the details yet, but it’s pretty exciting. Mitch Roberts, the founder and CEO of Evo and a 2022 Forbes 30 Under 30 honoree, said, “Today is a monumental moment for our company and our industry.” This deal makes Evo the biggest operator of cinema-entertainment centers in the country, which is a huge step forward for innovation in cinema. Both Mitch and Kevin Mitchell, the CEO and founder of Showbiz Cinemas, have plenty of experience in the cinema world. Their work in creating cinema entertainment centers has really paid off. Kevin mentioned, "It’s a wonderful time to hand over the company I started to a fourth-generation motion picture exhibitor and family member, especially since Showbiz Cinemas just had an amazing holiday season! I’m confident that with Mitch’s leadership and Marbella’s financial backing, Showbiz has a bright future ahead, and I’m excited to see what new adventures we’ll tackle together in and out of the entertainment scene."

Hey everyone! So, EVO Entertainment Group just made some big news by buying ShowBiz Cinemas in a cash deal that was announced on Monday, January 3rd. This marks the first big move since EVO teamed up with Marbella Interests, which is run by Bryan Sheffield, who’s got quite the background as the founder of Formentera Partners and the former CEO of Parsley Energy. Together, they’re combining forces to manage 16 venues with a whopping 148 screens, 108 bowling lanes, nine full-service restaurants and bars, a live music space that holds 3,000 people, plus over 30,000 square feet of gaming and attractions across Texas, Florida, Oklahoma, and Wyoming. EVO isn’t stopping here though; they’ve got more locations in the works for 2022! Mitch Roberts, the founder and CEO of EVO Entertainment Group, called this a huge moment for both the company and the industry, highlighting that this deal makes EVO the biggest operator of cinema-entertainment centers in the country, setting the stage for some exciting innovations in cinema. Kevin Mitchell, the CEO and Founder of ShowBiz Cinemas, expressed his excitement too, saying it feels great to pass the company he started to a fourth-generation motion picture exhibitor. He’s feeling optimistic about the future, especially after a record-breaking holiday season, and is looking forward to new adventures in and out of the entertainment world!

Hey there! So, guess what? EVO Entertainment Group just made a big move in the cinema world. They’ve officially acquired ShowBiz Cinemas, which means they now hold 100% of the company in a cash deal. This is pretty exciting news for both companies! Mitch Roberts, the Founder and CEO of EVO, shared that this is a huge moment for them and the entire industry. With this acquisition, EVO is now the largest operator of cinema-entertainment centers in the country! It’s really setting the stage for some cool innovations in cinema going forward. Plus, Mitch and Kevin Mitchell, the CEO and Founder of ShowBiz, have been around the cinema scene for ages, and their work in creating these entertainment centers has really taken off.

query: When does the 2022 Olympic Winter Games end?
positive: So, the 2022 Winter Olympics, officially known as the XXIV Olympic Winter Games, and often just called Beijing 2022, took place from February 4 to 20, 2022, in Beijing and the surrounding areas. Some events even kicked off a bit earlier on February 2. This was actually the 24th Winter Olympics! Beijing got the nod to host back in 2015 during the 128th IOC Session in Kuala Lumpur, which was pretty cool since it was their second time hosting the Olympics. Plus, it was the last of three straight Olympics held in East Asia, following the 2018 Winter Games in Pyeongchang and the 2020 Summer Games in Tokyo. Fun fact: after having hosted the 2008 Summer Olympics, Beijing became the first city to host both Summer and Winter Games!

The closing ceremony for the 2022 Winter Olympics took place at the Beijing National Stadium on February 20, 2022. It featured a cultural presentation, some closing remarks, and a formal handover to Milan and Cortina d'Ampezzo, who will host the 2026 Winter Olympics. This year's Winter Olympics were pretty exciting, with a record 109 events across 15 disciplines in seven sports! There were even seven new medal events added, like men’s and women’s big air freestyle, women’s monobob, and mixed team competitions in freestyle skiing aerials, ski jumping, snowboard cross, and a mixed relay in short track speed skating. By the way, the numbers in parentheses show how many medal events were contested in each discipline. Back in October 2016, the International Ski Federation (FIS) made a big announcement about allowing women to compete in Nordic combined for the first time at the Olympics in Beijing. Then, in November 2017, they proposed adding three more events for Olympic consideration: a mixed team ski jumping competition and men’s and women’s big air in freestyle skiing.

By Jaclyn Diaz

Hey there! So, during the closing ceremony on Day 16 of the Beijing 2022 Winter Olympics, there was this stunning fireworks display at the Beijing National Stadium on February 20, 2022. It was quite a sight! 

Now, let’s talk about the Olympics itself. It wrapped up after 16 days filled with 109 events, but it was a bit of a rollercoaster ride, right? With COVID-19 worries, some political drama, and a fresh Russian doping scandal, it definitely had its challenges. 

On that chilly evening at the Bird's Nest stadium, a small but lively crowd gathered to enjoy the show. Some fans even did the wave and proudly waved the Chinese flag while they waited for the festivities to kick off. Chinese President Xi Jinping was there too, sitting next to International Olympic Committee President Thomas Bach, taking it all in.

As for the performances, they were a bit on the simpler side, which kind of matched the quieter vibe of the opening ceremony earlier in the month. The organizers really focused on showcasing snow and ice as the main theme of the night.

So, Team Denmark was making their entrance into the Beijing National Stadium during the Closing Ceremony of the Beijing 2022 Winter Olympics on February 20, 2022. It was Day 16 of the games, and you could really feel the excitement in the air! 

Meanwhile, over at the Men's Cross-Country Skiing 50km Mass Start Free medal ceremony, you had gold medalist Alexander Bolshunov from Team ROC, right in the middle, with silver medalist Ivan Yakimushkin to his left and bronze medalist Simen Hegstad Krueger from Norway on the right, all proudly showing off their medals.

And let's not forget about the Olympic Cauldron! It was quite a sight as performers danced around it during the Closing Ceremony. Inside the stadium, the atmosphere was electric, with the cauldron shining bright, making it a memorable end to an incredible event.

So, the snow season kicked off in November and lasted a solid five months. During that time, Chongli was buzzing with activity, hosting thirty-six competitions and events, including the Far East Cup and the Children Skiing International Festival. They even set up twenty-three skiing camps, which attracted around 3,800 young skiers! All the venue construction started back in November 2016 and wrapped up by the end of 2020, just in time for the city to hold some test events. 

Oh, and on October 26, 2021, they unveiled the medal design for the Games, which is really cool because it draws inspiration from the medals of the 2008 Summer Olympics and mixes in some elements of Chinese astronomy and astrology, coinciding with the Chinese New Year celebrations. The uniforms for the folks presenting the medals were revealed in January 2022, created through a collaboration between the Central Academy of Fine Arts and the Beijing Institute of Fashion Technology.

The torch relay began in Greece on October 18, 2021, and it was announced that the local leg would kick off on February 2 and wrap up on February 4, 2022, during the Opening Ceremonies. The local leg was pretty short, only stopping in two cities: Beijing and Zhangjiakou. And just a heads up, there was a protest by activists at the Olympic torch lighting ceremony in Greece, which made some headlines too.

Hey there! So, did you know that the Beijing-Zhangjiakou high-speed railway, which was wrapped up in 2019, really sped things up? It now takes just 20 minutes to get from Beijing to Yanqing and only 50 minutes to Zhangjiakou. Pretty quick, right? Plus, the Beijing 2022 Winter Olympics added seven new events—like women’s monobob and freestyle skiing big air for both men and women, along with some mixed team events in short track speed skating, ski jumping, freestyle skiing aerials, and snowboard cross. This made it the most gender-balanced Winter Games ever, with a record number of female athletes at 45.44 percent! The Olympics kicked off in February 2022, right during the Chinese New Year, which is a huge deal in China. The locals really got into the spirit, welcoming athletes from all over the world and inviting them to enjoy the festivities, the stunning scenery, and the rich culture of Beijing, Yanqing, and Zhangjiakou. Oh, and don’t forget to check out the list of teams and the medals they won!

During the Beijing 2022 Winter Olympics Closing Ceremony on February 20, 2022, at the Beijing National Stadium, performers lit up the stage with their dance moves. It was quite the sight! We also saw children from Milan and Cortina holding a globe as part of the handover ceremony. And let’s not forget the iconic Olympic Cauldron making an appearance during the event. Oh, and just a quick note: there was a mix-up in a previous report that stated the U.S. came in third for total medal count, but actually, they finished fifth. Brian Mann helped with this report, by the way!

So, during the ceremony on Sunday, IOC leader Bach handed over the Olympic flag to the mayors of Milan and Cortino, who are set to host the 2026 Winter Games. You might remember seeing Xi Jinping, the President of China, on a big screen as the Olympic flag was waving before the closing ceremony of the Beijing 2022 Winter Olympics. That event took place at the National Stadium, also known as the Bird's Nest, on February 20, 2022. 

There were some adorable kids carrying snowflake lanterns who performed during the closing ceremony, adding a special touch to the whole thing. The atmosphere was really festive, especially with other children also taking to the stage to perform. And let's not forget the flag bearers making their way into the stadium, adding to the excitement of the day. What a memorable way to wrap up the games!

So, here's the deal: the IOC wanted total control over all the advertising space in Oslo, but that’s a bit tricky because the Norwegian government doesn’t actually own all that space. They can't just hand it over to a foreign organization like the IOC for exclusive use. A bunch of commentators have called out this kind of demand as pretty out there for a western democracy. One publication even called the IOC a "notoriously ridiculous organization run by grifters and hereditary aristocrats." Ole Berget, who’s the deputy minister in the Finance Ministry, mentioned that a lot of folks were really taken aback by the IOC's arrogance. Norway’s biggest newspaper weighed in too, saying, "Sure, we’re a rich country, but we’re not about to waste our money satisfying the crazy demands from IOC officials. They can’t expect to be treated like royalty here—it just won’t work with the Norwegian public." On another note, Beijing snagged the 2022 Winter Olympics as the host city, beating out Almaty by just four votes during the IOC Session in Kuala Lumpur back in July 2015. positive: Hey there! So, did you hear about the awesome news from Georgia Tech? On January 21, 2022, Ford Greene, Ralph Long Jr., and Lawrence Williams, who were the first Black students at the school, along with Ronald Yancey, the first Black graduate, are set to receive the 2022 Ivan Allen Jr. Prize for Social Courage. This prize has been around since 2010, created to honor Ivan Allen Jr., who was not only a Georgia Tech alumnus but also a former mayor of Atlanta. Thanks to the Wilbur and Hilda Glenn Family Foundation, the prize comes with a cool $100,000 stipend for the winners! The first prize was given out back in March 2011, and it's all about recognizing those brave leaders who, like Mayor Allen, take personal risks to help others. Georgia Tech President Ángel Cabrera shared that Greene, Long, Williams, and Yancey faced all kinds of challenges to show the world that Black students deserved a place at Georgia Tech, opening doors for so many Black Yellow Jackets who followed in their footsteps. By the way, Greene, Long, and Williams are often called the “three pioneers,” and you can see their legacy in a sculpture at Harrison Square that shows them on their first day at Tech, which was back in fall 1961. While their arrival didn’t spark violence like it did at some other southern schools, they definitely had their share of struggles.

You know, it’s pretty cool that the House of Representatives John Lewis and former U.S. President, and Tech alum, Jimmy Carter are just a couple of names in the mix. It’s not every day that the nomination committee and the Tech President decide to give the award to a student from the Institute, but it’s a fitting move, especially since it marks 60 years since Black students started attending Tech. The Institute really gets that its diversity is what makes it strong. Think about Ford Greene, Ralph Long Jr., the late Lawrence Williams, and Ronald Yancey—they really made a difference back in their day, and you can still feel their courage in the campus vibe today. Their efforts have opened doors for so many Black Yellow Jackets who came after them. By the way, there’s an award symposium to celebrate these four pioneers happening on April 20, 2022, from 10 a.m. to 2 p.m. at the Biltmore Ballroom in Tech Square.

Hey there! So, here’s some exciting news: Ford Greene, Ralph Long Jr., Lawrence Williams—who were Georgia Tech’s first Black students—and Ronald Yancey, the school’s first Black graduate, are all set to receive the 2022 Ivan Allen Jr. Prize for Social Courage. You might have seen their faces in those campus sculptures, but now, years after they broke the color barrier at Georgia Tech, they’re joining an impressive lineup of past honorees like Jimmy and Rosalynn Carter, John Lewis, Andrew Young, and other incredible leaders. Georgia Tech President Ángel Cabrera mentioned that the Ivan Allen Jr. Prize is all about recognizing brave leaders who take personal risks to make life better for others. He highlighted how Greene, Long, Williams, and Yancey faced all sorts of challenges to show the world that Black students belonged at Georgia Tech, paving the way for so many Black Yellow Jackets who’ve graduated since then. By the way, Greene, Long, and Williams, known as the “three pioneers” in the sculpture at Harrison Square, kicked off their classes in the fall of 1961. While their arrival didn’t spark violence like it did at some other southern schools, it definitely wasn’t a walk in the park.

Back in 1961, three Black students kicked off their classes at Georgia Tech, making a big splash in history as the first higher education institution in the Deep South to integrate peacefully without needing a court order. At that time, college campuses across the South were buzzing with civil unrest. To honor their groundbreaking efforts, Tech’s first Black students — Lawrence Williams, Ralph Long Jr., and the late Ford Greene, along with the first Black graduate Ronald Yancey — received the 2022 Ivan Allen Jr. Prize for Social Courage on April 20 at the Biltmore Ballroom in Atlanta. During the celebration, there was a symposium led by 2020 Ivan Allen Jr. Prize winner Charlayne Hunter-Gault, where Long Jr., Williams, and Yancey shared their experiences about integrating the Institute and talked about the ongoing fight for racial equity and inclusion in higher education everywhere. They opened up about the racism they faced, even though it looked like integration was going smoothly on the surface. Their experiences often left them feeling a bit lonely and disconnected from most of the other students back then, but they also expressed hope that future Black students at Tech would feel right at home on campus. “I’m so thankful for those who continue to matriculate here and carry on the tradition,” Williams said. Oh, and there was even a video greeting from Atlanta Mayor Andre Dickens, who’s a Georgia Tech chemical engineering grad himself, adding a nice touch to the celebration!

query: What is the codename for Google's AR headset project?
positive: Back in January 2022, The Verge shared some exciting news: Google was working on an AR headset known as Project Iris. This cool gadget was designed to use outward-facing cameras to mix computer graphics with real-world video. They were keeping it all under wraps at a super secure facility in the San Francisco Bay Area. The project was led by Bavor and was set to run on Android, plus a custom chip similar to Tensor, with a launch planned for 2024, right alongside Project Starline. Some notable folks working on this included Shahram Izadi, Eddie Chung, Scott Huffman, Kurt Akeley, and Lucovsky. Interestingly, the early prototype looked a lot like the North Focals. Then, in March, The Information reported that Google was set to buy Raxium, an AR hardware startup, for around $1 billion. They had plans to keep acquiring more companies to boost their AR and mixed reality efforts, and they wrapped up the acquisition just a month later. Fast forward to May 2022, Google revealed a new version of Iris that looked like glasses and could do live translations during the 2022 Google I/O keynote. By August, they started testing these prototypes publicly in the U.S., and by October, they expanded the testing to Canada. However, it seems that the constantly shifting strategy for Project Iris was frustrating for some employees at Google, according to Business Insider.

It’s their big play in the Augmented Reality (AR) space, aiming to give some stiff competition to what Microsoft, Apple, and even the Metaverse are cooking up. Remember when Google was all about the DayDream VR headsets and Google Glasses? Well, it looks like the surge in AR tech has shifted their focus. Now, they’re stepping into the AR arena alongside Facebook, Microsoft, and Apple. 

The new headsets, dubbed Project Iris, might feature some cool outer camera tech that blends real-world visuals with computer graphics. Plus, these headsets are designed to be completely wireless, meaning no annoying cables to deal with! Reports suggest they’ll be powered by the same type of Silicon chipset found in last year’s Pixel 6 smartphone. 

Visually, these AR headsets resemble ski goggles, complete with cameras, and they don’t need an external power source. The specific chipset in play is the GS101, but for those stunning graphics, they’ll tap into data centers to pull in the heavy visuals, so you’ll need an internet connection to keep everything running smoothly. Oh, and Google has shared that there’s a whole team dedicated to making this project happen. Exciting stuff, right?

So, Project Iris was this cool name for an augmented reality headset that Google was working on. It was designed to look just like regular glasses and was supposed to come out in 2024, but they ended up scrapping it in early 2023. After the whole mess with Google Glass back in 2013—where it really didn’t go over well—Google was pretty hesitant to dive back into wearable AR tech. But by 2021, they started tinkering with a new headset, which turned out to be Project Iris. Now, it’s worth noting that the game wasn’t available in Belgium and the Netherlands at first because of those strict loot box rules, but the Netherlands eventually changed its mind. Can you believe that within just twenty-four hours of its launch, Lost Ark became the second most played game on Steam? Pretty impressive, right? Back in 2021, there were talks between Smilegate and the publisher HappyTuk about bringing Lost Ark to Taiwan, but in 2023, we heard that the release for Taiwanese servers is on hold, although it hasn't been scrapped entirely. As for reviews, Lost Ark got a "generally favorable" rating on Metacritic. PC Magazine even raved about the combat, saying it looks great, sounds powerful, and feels amazing to play. You really get that sense of being a combat god when you divekick into a crowd and turn those monsters into a bloody mess!

Lost Ark is this really cool MMO action role-playing game that was co-developed by Tripod Studio and Smilegate, and it hit the scene back in 2019. It launched in South Korea first in December that year, and then it made its way to Europe, North America, and South America in February 2022, thanks to Amazon Games. Can you believe it cost around $85 million to develop? Pretty wild, right? The game got a lot of positive reviews and has become one of the most-played titles on Steam. 

In Lost Ark, the main focus is on player versus environment (PvE) stuff and exploring, like questing, hunting for achievements and collectibles, and crafting. There's also some player versus player (PvP) action thrown in there. You kick things off by customizing your character, and then you can level them up by diving into the storyline. Once you hit level 50, you gain access to some tiered endgame dungeons and raids, and from there, it’s all about boosting your character’s gear score to keep progressing. 

Fun fact: the game’s development started way back in 2011 under the name Project T. It runs on Unreal Engine 3 and initially only supported DirectX 9, but they updated it to also work with DirectX 11 before the western release in 2022.

query: What medals did Jessie Diggins win in the Beijing 2022 Olympic Games?
positive: Hey, did you hear about Jessie Diggins? She just pulled off an incredible feat at the 2022 Beijing Winter Games! After battling food poisoning just 30 hours earlier, this Stratton-trained cross-country skier snagged her second medal, taking silver in the 30-kilometer freestyle race. Can you believe she also won bronze in the individual sprint on February 8? She told NBC, “It’s really emotional. That was one of the hardest things I’ve ever done in my whole life, especially because I thought I was going to die at the finish line with my legs cramping for the last 17 kilometers.” With this latest win, Jessie now has an Olympic medal in every color, having previously won gold in the 2018 women’s team sprint. She shared, “It’s been an emotional roller-coaster, but I am so happy we made it to the end. To have a medal in the sprint and the 30K are the ultimate bookends for me.” She finished the race in 1 hour 26 minutes 37 seconds, coming in just under two minutes behind Norway’s Therese Johaug and 50 seconds ahead of Finland’s Kerttu Niskanen. What an amazing journey for her!

You won't believe it, but Jessie Diggins just snagged a silver medal in the 30km mass start at the Beijing 2022 Olympics! That makes her the first American cross-country skier to win two medals at one Games. How cool is that? At 30 years old, she really cemented her status as the best cross-country skier the USA has ever seen. She finished second to Norway's Therese Johaug, who took home her third individual title in Beijing, rounding out her country's medal tally to 25. Jessie had already scored a bronze in the individual sprint, making her the first American woman to medal in cross-country, so this silver really adds to her amazing achievements! And let's not forget, she made history before by winning Team USA's first gold in the sport alongside Kikkan Randall in the 2018 PyeongChang team sprint. The wild part? Just before her final race, she was dealing with food poisoning! She mentioned to Olympics.com that she wasn't feeling too confident going into the race, saying, “If I'm being honest, 24 hours ago I felt really bad and I just laid in bed yesterday mostly and ate food and drank sport drinks.” But it seems like that extra rest did the trick! After crossing the finish line, she actually collapsed—what a race!

Hey there! So, here’s the scoop from Beijing: Jessie Diggins snagged the U.S.'s 25th and final medal at the 2022 Olympic Games, taking home a silver in this super tough 30km mass start cross-country race. And get this—she was battling through exhaustion and the aftermath of food poisoning! After she crossed the finish line and collapsed, she said, “That might have been the best race of my entire life, I'm not going to lie. It was also maybe the hardest race of my whole life." The race wrapped up the events in the Zhangjiakou mountain area, and they got pretty lucky with the weather—14 degrees and those beautiful slate-blue skies that have been around for most of the Games. With stars and stripes on her hat, arms, and legs, Jessie really pushed through. For a big chunk of the race, she was all alone—over a minute behind Norway’s Therese Johaug, who took gold, and also over a minute ahead of everyone else. Jessie, who surprised everyone with her gold in the team sprint back in PyeongChang, was super busy this time around. She competed in all six cross-country events and managed to finish in the top 8 every single time—pretty impressive, right? On top of her silver, she also grabbed a bronze in the sprint!

At the Games, Roque notched up a goal and two assists, finishing with a +4 for the tournament. The U.S. team snagged the silver medal after a close match against Canada, ending 3-2. Fast forward to February 20, 2022: just four years after helping the U.S. snag its first gold in cross-country skiing, Diggins racked up two medals in 2022, making her the most decorated U.S. cross-country skier ever! “Honestly, that might have been the best race of my whole life,” Diggins shared, sounding pretty emotional. She mentioned how her teammates from Team USA were cheering her on, especially a bunch of folks from Vermont involved in skiing and biathlon. “It felt like everyone was right there with me, especially when things got tough—everyone was just breathing alongside me,” she said, wiping away tears. “We had so much support and love out there, and I just want to say thank you.” Last year, Diggins made history as the first American to snag an overall World Cup title, and in Beijing, she competed in six cross-country events, finishing in the top 8 for all of them! Not only is she the first U.S. woman to earn a solo Nordic cross-country medal, but she’s also just the second American ever to do it, following Vermont's Bill Koch who won silver back in 1976. With her latest win, she became the first American to stand on multiple cross-country podiums at the same Winter Games. As the competitions in Beijing wrap up, Olympians with connections to Vermont have racked up a total of six medals: On Feb. 8, Ryan Cochran-Siegle took home silver and Diggins grabbed bronze. Then on Feb. 9, Lindsey Jacobellis, who came from Vermont, scored the first U.S. gold medal of the 2022 Olympics. She followed that up on Feb. 12 with another gold! And don’t forget Megan Nick, the aerial skier from Shelburne, who won bronze in her Olympic debut on Feb. 14. What an exciting time!

“Luckily, I’ve spent the last decade training in Alaska,” Brennan, 33, shared, as she competed in her second Olympics. “We’ve had some really tough training days, so I just kept reminding myself that I’m tough enough to get through it all and that everyone else is out there dealing with the same challenges.” Diggins snagged a silver medal, marking the 25th Olympic medal for the U.S. team in Beijing, which beats the 23 medals the Americans won four years ago in Pyeongchang. This is Diggins’ third Olympic medal, solidifying her as the most successful American skier in Olympic history, with gold, silver, and bronze to her credit. She also made history as the first non-European skier to earn an Olympic medal in the women’s 30K. Four years back, she teamed up with Kikkan Randall in the team sprint and became the first Americans to win Olympic gold in cross-country skiing. The only other American to win an individual Olympic medal is Bill Koch, who took home silver in the men’s 30K at the 1976 Innsbruck Olympics. Diggins previously won a bronze on February 8 in the women’s sprint, marking her first individual Olympic medal. But then on February 16, she had a tough time in the team sprint with Brennan, mentioning she wasn’t feeling well and struggled toward the end.

query: When was Elden Ring being released?
positive: You know that golden light or aura you see in their eyes? That’s a sign of the blessing from the Erdtree. But over time, some folks lost that grace, and the light in their eyes dimmed. These people are called the Tarnished. They were kicked out and exiled from the game’s world long before you, the player, step in. Since then, the Elden Ring has been shattered, and guess what? The Tarnished are being summoned back to The Lands Between. So, what are you waiting for? Pre-order now, and we’ll catch you on the flip side… Elden Ring drops on February 25, 2022, for PS4, PS5, Xbox One, Xbox Series X, and PC. Keep checking back for more updates! So, Elden Ring drops on February 25, 2022, and it’ll be available on PS4, PS5, Xbox One, Xbox Series X, and PC. If you pre-order it on Xbox One or PS4, you’ll snag a free upgrade to the next-gen version, which is pretty sweet! The game’s priced at £59.99 in the UK and $59.99 in the US. 

Now, if you’re into collector’s items, there’s a collector’s edition for £189.99, and it comes with the game, a cool steelbook, a 40-page art book, a digital soundtrack, and a 9-inch statue of Malenia – Blade of Miquella. And if you really want to go all out, there’s a premium collector’s edition for a whopping £259.99, which includes everything I just mentioned plus a replica helmet of Malenia. You can pre-order any of the editions, including the standard one, over at Bandai Namco’s official website. 

Just a heads up, the story takes place in the Lands Between, where the Elden Ring, the source of the Erdtree, has been shattered, and it’s causing all sorts of chaos in the realm.

So, here’s the scoop: Bandai Namco kicked off playtesting for Elden Ring back in November 2021 with a closed beta that players could sign up for. The game was originally set to launch on January 21, 2022, but then it got pushed back to February 25 instead. When it finally dropped, there were some hiccups right at the start; players were noticing issues with the frame rate. Bandai Namco didn’t just sit back, though—they rolled out some patches and updates to fix things up. 

Fast forward to November 2022, and we saw a crowdfunding campaign for an Elden Ring-themed board game pop up on Kickstarter, courtesy of Steamforged Games. Then, in February 2023, they announced an expansion called Shadow of the Erdtree, although we’ll have to wait a bit longer for that one. 

As for the game itself, it really hit the mark with players, earning "universal acclaim" on Metacritic. It was a hit on Twitch too, racking up nearly 900,000 viewers in just 24 hours, making it the third-biggest launch after Lost Ark and Cyberpunk 2077. People loved the open-world setting, and reviewers were all about the exploration mechanics. Tamoor Hussain from GameSpot even said the Lands Between was the most expansive world FromSoftware has created, highlighting how exploration and discovery are the game’s biggest draws. Mitchell Saltzman over at IGN also praised Elden Ring for rewarding players who dive into every corner of the map.

So, have you heard about Elden Ring? It’s this awesome action role-playing game that came out in 2022, developed by FromSoftware. Hidetaka Miyazaki directed it, and they even got George R. R. Martin to help with the worldbuilding, which is pretty cool! It hit the shelves on February 25 for PlayStation 4, PlayStation 5, Windows, Xbox One, and Xbox Series X/S, with Bandai Namco Entertainment handling the international release.

In Elden Ring, you get to customize your own character and go on this epic quest to fix the Elden Ring and become the new Elden Lord. You play from a third-person perspective, and the open world is super interactive—it's really fun to just roam around. There are six main areas, and you travel around on your trusty steed, Torrent. Plus, there are these hidden dungeons you can explore to snag some useful items.

You can choose from a bunch of weapons and magic spells, and they even have stealth mechanics if you want to take a sneaky approach. As you explore, you’ll find checkpoints that let you fast travel and upgrade your character using Runes, which is the in-game currency. And if you’re into multiplayer, there’s an online mode where you can team up with friends or battle it out in player-versus-player combat.

When they were planning this game, FromSoftware aimed to create an open-world experience that still felt like Dark Souls, making Elden Ring a natural evolution of the series. Miyazaki really admired Martin’s work and believed his input would help craft a story that’s a bit more accessible than what we’ve seen in previous games.

So, February 13, 2022, is when the buzz starts because Elden Ring is set to drop on February 25, 2022! It's going to be available on PS4, PS5, Xbox One, Xbox Series X, and PC. And hey, if you’re thinking about pre-ordering it on the Xbox One or...

Now, let’s take a quick peek at her Champions Corner. Remember her surreal run at Indian Wells? In 2022, she racked up 20 wins, including a 6-4, 6-1 victory over Maria Sakkari in the final, making her the player with the most wins on tour that year. 

Thanks to her back-to-back WTA 1000 titles, she hit a new career-high ranking, which is super exciting! And did you know she pulled off 3 comebacks from a set down at Indian Wells? She managed to turn things around against Anhelina Kalinina, Clara Tauson, and Angelique Kerber in her first three matches. In total, she had seven such comebacks in 2022, which is impressive considering she only had eight in her entire career before this season! Oh, and she’s on a roll with 11 consecutive wins, tying with the then No. 1 player. What a year for Swiatek!

So, back in January 2022, Świątek was on quite a roll! She made it to the second week of six straight Grand Slams, kicking things off with her French Open title in 2020. That’s a record for the longest streak of reaching the second week! In the quarterfinals, she had a tough match against Kaia Kanepi that lasted over three hours, but she ended up losing to Danielle Collins in the semifinals the next day. 

Then in February, she joined the Qatar Open as the seventh seed. She took down Viktorija Golubic and Daria Kasatkina in the second and third rounds, which set her up for a quarterfinal showdown against top seed Aryna Sabalenka, whom she beat! In the semifinals, she faced Maria Sakkari and won in straight sets. The final was a breeze for her against fourth-seeded Anett Kontaveit; she only dropped two games to snag her second WTA 1000 championship title. 

Later, at Indian Wells, she made it to her third WTA 1000 final. As the third seed, she had some tough matches against Anhelina Kalinina, Clara Tauson, and Angelique Kerber, all in three sets, plus straight-set victories over Madison Keys and Simona Halep. She wrapped it up by beating sixth seed Maria Sakkari, again in straight sets, to earn her fifth title! With those back-to-back Masters wins, Świątek hit a career-high ranking of world No. 2. So, back in March 2021, she made it into the top 15 for the first time ever! Seeded 15th, Świątek snagged her first WTA 1000 title at the Italian Open, and get this—she took down former champion Karolína Plíšková in just 46 minutes without losing a single game. Talk about impressive! She made it to the final by beating two-time champion and world No. 5 Elina Svitolina, plus the second-best teenager, Coco Gauff, all in one day. Oh, and she saved match points in her third-round match against Barbora Krejčíková, too. query: Who won the Male Vocalist of the Year at the 2022 CMA ?
positive: Hey there! So, Chris Stapleton snagged the Male Vocalist of the Year trophy at the 2022 CMA Awards, beating out some tough competition like Eric Church, Luke Combs, Cody Johnson, and Morgan Wallen. You could really see how much it meant to him, and during his acceptance speech, he took a moment to thank everyone who's been there for him throughout his journey. He said, "This is a dream every minute we get to live this," and added, "I'm evidence that dreams come true all the time, so thank you, thank you to everybody." Pretty touching, right? This win marks his second year in a row taking home the Male Vocalist of the Year award and his sixth time overall! He had a great run from 2015 to 2018 but lost the title to Combs for a couple of years in 2019 and 2020. But hey, in 2022, his classic style and consistent performances really shined through. Plus, with his All-American Road Show, he's been one of the busiest and most reliable touring acts in country music this year. Oh, and did you know he’s also featured in a current exhibit at the Country Music Hall of Fame? Earlier this year, he even picked up three Grammy Awards, including Best Country Album for his 2020 project, Starting Over. The 2022 CMA Awards were broadcast live from Nashville's Bridgestone Arena on ABC, and it was quite the night!

Luke Combs snagged the big one, Entertainer of the Year, for the second time, and he also picked up his second Album of the Year trophy for Growin’ Up. Chris Stapleton was on fire too, winning his sixth Male Vocalist of the Year award, which makes him the fifth most-winning artist in CMA history, right after legends like Brooks & Dunn and George Strait. Plus, he’s now the all-time champ in the Male Vocalist category! 

Lainey Wilson, who was up for a bunch of awards that night, took home both New Artist of the Year and Female Vocalist of the Year. That puts her in an exclusive club with Alison Krauss and Carrie Underwood as the only artists to win both in the same year! Jordan Davis also had a great night, winning Song of the Year for his duet “Buy Dirt” with CMA host Luke Bryan. He co-wrote that hit with his brother Jacob and the talented Jenkins brothers, Josh and Matt. 

Cody Johnson won Single of the Year for “‘Til You Can’t” and snagged Music Video of the Year for that song even before the show started. And let’s not forget Brothers Osborne, who took home Vocal Duo of the Year for the sixth time, while Old Dominion celebrated their fifth win for Vocal Group of the Year. What a night for country music!

query: Who won the ACC Tournament in 2022?
positive: So, the 2022 ACC men's basketball tournament, officially known as the 2022 New York Life ACC Men's Basketball Tournament because of sponsorship stuff, was the big postseason event for the 2021–22 Atlantic Coast Conference men's basketball season. It took place at the Barclays Center in Brooklyn, New York, from March 8 to 12, 2022. This was actually the 69th time they held the tournament. The Virginia Tech Hokies came out on top, snagging their first-ever ACC Tournament title and just their second conference tournament title in the program's history, which also earned them an automatic spot in the 2022 NCAA tournament. They made history as the second ACC champion to win four games in the tournament to claim the title, and they were the lowest seed ever to do so. Every single ACC team joined in on the tournament fun, with teams being seeded based on their conference records, plus a tiebreaker system for teams that ended up with the same records. Duke ended up with the regular-season title and the top overall seed. Notre Dame, North Carolina, and Miami also got double-byes. Oh, and just to note: Hunter Cattoor from Virginia Tech was named the tournament MVP!

Hey there! So, Apple kicked off pre-orders on September 9, and you could actually get your hands on the iPhone 14 starting September 16, with the iPhone 14 Plus following a bit later on October 7. If you’re wondering about the design, it’s pretty much the same as the iPhone 13, but keep in mind that the US versions don’t come with a physical SIM tray anymore. 

Now, when it comes to colors, you’ve got six options to choose from: Blue, Purple, Midnight, Starlight, Yellow, and Product Red. Oh, and Purple is a fresh addition, swapping out the Pink from the iPhone 13 and iPhone 13 Mini. Just so you know, they added that cheerful yellow color on March 7, 2023. 

As for storage, the iPhone 14 and 14 Plus come in three sizes: 128, 256, and 512 GB. Both models pack in 6 GB of RAM, which is a nice upgrade from the 4 GB you’d find in the previous iPhone 13 and 13 Mini. Plus, they still have the same solid IP68 rating for dust and water resistance that you got with the older models. 

Under the hood, the iPhone 14 and 14 Plus are powered by Apple’s own A15 Bionic chip, which has a 6-core CPU, 5-core GPU, and a 16-core Neural Engine. Just a heads-up, the iPhone 14 Pro and 14 Pro Max come with the even faster A16 Bionic chip if you're looking for something speedier!

So, it looks like Apple has decided not to give the regular iPhone a new processor this year. Instead, the iPhone 14 and 14 Plus will run on the A15 Bionic chip, which you might remember is the same one found in the iPhone 13 Pro. But here’s the kicker: it’s got a little extra power compared to the A15 in the standard iPhone 13 and the $429 iPhone SE, thanks to an additional GPU core. This marks the first time since the 2008 iPhone 3G that Apple hasn’t updated the processor for one of its flagship models, which had almost the same internals as the original iPhone. On the flip side, the iPhone 14 Pro is getting the A16 processor, which Apple claims is more power-efficient, even when you’re putting it through some heavy tasks. The specs are pretty similar, though—like the A15 in the regular 14, it features a six-core CPU, a five-core GPU, and a 16-core Neural Engine. Apple says the new version just has better cores. As for why they chose not to update the chipset for the standard iPhone 14, it’s not too shocking. Analysts like Ming-Chi Kuo and reporters like Mark Gurman have been hinting at this for a while, and the chip shortage is still a concern. Sticking with chips that are already tried and tested could help ensure that there are enough processors to keep up with demand.

Hey there! So, Apple’s gearing up to show off the brand new iPhone 14 lineup this fall, and it’s got some cool features in store, like an always-on display and a better camera. Now, let’s talk about the iPhone 14 chip—this year’s gonna be a bit different! For the first time, the chip in the iPhone 14 will be different from the one in the iPhone 14 Pro. Just to recap, we’re expecting four models this time around, with two screen sizes. The iPhone 14 and iPhone 14 Pro will have a 6.1-inch display, while the iPhone 14 Max and iPhone 14 Pro Max will sport a 6.7-inch display. This is a big deal because it’s the first time Apple will offer a non-Pro iPhone with that size screen! But there will be some key differences between the regular iPhone 14 models and the Pro versions, especially when it comes to the chip. Apple usually upgrades the chip every year—like the iPhone 12 had the A14 chip and the iPhone 13 had the A15. But this year, it looks like the iPhone 14 and iPhone 14 Max won’t get that upgrade. According to the well-known Apple analyst Ming-Chi Kuo, they’re sticking with the same chip. Interesting, right?

You know what's interesting? The iPhone 14 actually uses the same chip as the A15 in last year's iPhone 13 Pro and Pro Max. This one has more memory and an extra GPU core compared to the A15 in the regular iPhone 13 models. It's kind of a big deal because the iPhone 14 is the first flagship model since the 2008 iPhone 3G that hasn’t switched up its chip from the previous year. According to Mitchell Clark from The Verge, this was probably a move to keep costs in check during the ongoing chip shortage and inflation. He also mentioned that the A15 still beats out the newer chips from Qualcomm and Google in most tests, but using an older chip might mean the iPhone 14 doesn’t get as many updates down the line, much like what happened with the iPhone 5C back in 2013. 

As for the displays, the iPhone 14 comes with a 6.1-inch Super Retina XDR OLED screen, boasting a resolution of 2532 × 1170 pixels and a pixel density of about 460 PPI, plus a refresh rate of 60 Hz. If you go for the iPhone 14 Plus, you’ll get a bigger 6.7-inch display with the same awesome technology, but with a resolution of 2778 × 1284 pixels and a pixel density of around 458 PPI. Both models can shine bright with typical brightness up to 800 nits and can hit a max brightness of 1200 nits. Pretty cool, right?

You know, that older chip might actually help keep prices down, especially with inflation making other gadgets more expensive. If you're thinking about getting an iPhone 14, you probably don’t need to stress too much about performance — the A15 chip still beats out the latest from Qualcomm and Google in most tests. But there’s still that nagging question about how long it will last. Apple usually does a solid job with software updates for their phones, but I can’t help but wonder if the iPhone 14’s chip will mean it gets one or two fewer iOS updates. Without any major advancements in manufacturing that shrink transistors, it’s tougher for designers to pack in features that increase the total number of transistors, which means we might miss out on upgrades like more graphics processing units and better support for AI software.

So, here’s the scoop: Warner Bros just dropped the news that they’re not going to release the Batgirl movie, which stars Leslie Grace as Barbara Gordon, along with big names like Michael Keaton, JK Simmons, and Brendan Fraser. Even though they wrapped up filming and were in post-production, they’ve decided it’s a no-go. This film was directed by Adil El Arbi and Bilall Fallah, who also worked on Ms Marvel, and it was supposed to be part of Warner Bros' push to make movies for HBO Max back in 2021. But on Tuesday, the studio confirmed that Batgirl won’t hit theaters or even stream on HBO Max. They didn’t really give any more details. The movie was set to feature Keaton back in his Batman role, with Grace as the lead, Simmons as Commissioner Gordon, and Fraser as the villain Firefly. According to The Hollywood Reporter, the budget ended up being a big factor in this decision, climbing to almost $90 million because of the expenses from filming during the pandemic. While that’s less than the typical DC superhero film, they felt it just didn’t have the kind of spectacle that fans expect, and it probably wouldn’t make back its costs if they did release it.

So, here’s the scoop: the New York Post, which was the first to break the news on Tuesday, talked to a source who didn’t want to be named. They mentioned that the budget actually went over $100 million, and early test screenings went so badly that Warner Bros decided it was time to cut their losses. The source even said, “They think an unspeakable Batgirl is going to be irredeemable.” Right after the article came out, I got a call from someone at a rival studio who was just shocked by the whole thing. They said, "I've been in this business for thirty years, and this is some unprecedented stuff right here." This decision puts the film in the running for one of the most expensive canceled projects ever. It’s all happening during a leadership shake-up since Warner Bros merged with Discovery back in May 2021.
