query: What is the name of China's rover on Mars?
positive: So, let’s talk about Tianwen-1’s rover, which is called Zhurong. This name comes from a figure in Chinese mythology who’s often linked with fire and light. Pretty cool, right? They actually picked the name through an online poll that ran from January to February 2021. Now, a bit of backstory: China’s Mars program actually kicked off in collaboration with Russia. Back in November 2011, they launched the Russian spacecraft Fobos-Grunt from the Baikonur Cosmodrome, aiming for Mars and its moon Phobos. Along for the ride was Yinghuo-1, which was supposed to be China’s first Mars orbiter. It even carried some experiments from the Bulgarian Academy of Sciences and the American Planetary Society. But here’s the twist: Fobos-Grunt had a major hiccup when its main propulsion unit failed, so it couldn’t leave Earth’s orbit. Unfortunately, it all came crashing back down to Earth in January 2012. After that, in 2014, China decided to go solo with its Mars project. They developed a brand new spacecraft that includes an orbiter, a lander, and a rover, all thanks to the China Aerospace Science and Technology Corporation. The National Space Science Centre in Beijing is managing the whole mission, which officially got the green light in 2016.

Hey there! So, if you want to get in on some extra perks, make sure to CONNECT WITH US! On April 25, 2021, at 5:06 pm IST, we heard some exciting news from Beijing. Check this out: there’s this cool artist's rendering that was shared by China’s Xinhua News Agency back on August 23, 2016, showing what the Chinese Mars rover and lander might look like. The big news is that China’s first Mars rover has been named Zhurong, after a traditional fire god, and this announcement came just recently. The rover is riding along on the Tianwen-1 probe, which made it to Mars orbit on February 24 and is set to land in May to search for signs of life. This is all part of China’s ambitious space plans, which also include launching a crewed orbital station and even landing a human on the moon! Remember back in 2019 when China became the first country to land a space probe on the far side of the moon? And they brought back lunar rocks to Earth in December, marking the first time since the 1970s! The name Zhurong really fits with what the Chinese call Mars, “Huo Xing,” or fire star, as the China National Space Administration explained. It’s all about “igniting the flame of China’s planetary exploration,” according to Wu Yanhua, a deputy administrator over at CNSA, who was quoted by the official Xinhua News Agency. How cool is that?

So, back in 2020, they were planning to send a probe to orbit Mars and even try landing on the planet. On August 23, 2016, the China National Space Administration (CNSA) shared the first images of the spacecraft for the mission, which showed that it was going to include an orbiter, a lander, and a rover—all in one go! They laid out the scientific goals and what they'd be carrying on their mission in a paper published in the Journal of Deep Space Exploration in December 2017. Fast forward to April 24, 2020, and CNSA officially announced their interplanetary exploration program, giving it the name Tianwen and even designing a cool emblem for it. The first mission, which was set to happen in 2020, was called Tianwen-1. Then, on April 24, 2021, as they geared up for the landing, they revealed that the rover would be named Zhurong. They even showcased a mockup of the Zhurong rover at the 69th International Astronautical Congress! To get everything just right, CNSA built a test rover to simulate conditions at Utopia Planitia, all set up at the China Academy of Space Technology in Beijing.

So, there's this six-wheel solar-powered rover that looks a bit like a blue butterfly and weighs around 240 kg. It’s packed with some cool tech: a Terrain Camera, a Multispectral Camera, a Mars-Rover Subsurface Exploration Radar, a Mars Surface Composition Detector, a Mars Magnetic Field Detector, and a Mars Meteorology Monitor. The name for China’s first Mars rover was revealed by the China National Space Administration (CNSA) in Nanjing, which is in east China's Jiangsu Province, on April 24, 2021. Back in July, they kicked off a global campaign to name the rover, inviting people from all over to vote for their favorite name from a list of ten candidates between January 20 and February 28. It’s a special date because it marks the launch of their first satellite, "Dongfanghong-1," back in 1970. This year, Nanjing hosted the main events for Space Day, and it’s really become a great way for both the Chinese public and the world to catch up on all the exciting stuff happening in China’s aerospace field.

So, on May 14, 2021, the lander and rover of the mission made a successful landing on Mars. This made China the third country ever to pull off a soft landing and communicate from the Martian surface, joining the ranks of the Soviet Union and the United States. Then, just a week later, on May 22, 2021, the Zhurong rover rolled out onto the Martian surface using the descent ramps from its landing platform. With that, China became the second nation to achieve this, right after the U.S. Plus, it's pretty cool that China is the first country to successfully orbit, land, and rover on Mars on its very first try! The Tianwen-1 mission also made history by being the second one to capture audio recordings on the Martian surface, following the U.S. Perseverance rover. The Zhurong rover even deployed a "smallsat" on Mars, which included a "drop camera" that took pictures of both the rover and the Tianwen-1 lander. And get this—the remote camera from Tianwen-1 weighs less than 1 kg, making it the lightest man-made object on Mars as of May 2021!

So, let’s talk about Zhurong! This little guy is a rover from China that made its way to Mars, making it the first Chinese rover to land on another planet after they sent two rovers to the Moon. Zhurong is part of the Tianwen-1 mission, which is run by the China National Space Administration (CNSA). The mission kicked off with a launch on July 23, 2020, and the spacecraft reached Mars orbit on February 10, 2021. Then, on May 14, 2021, the lander touched down on Mars, which was a big deal because it made China the third country ever to land a spacecraft on Mars and the second to send a rover there, right after the U.S. Zhurong rolled out on May 22, 2021, at 02:40 UTC. It was built to last about 90 sols, or 93 Earth days, but it’s been going strong for 347 sols, which is around 356.5 days, since it landed. However, it’s been quiet since May 20, 2022, mainly because of some nasty sandstorms and the Martian winter. We’re all just waiting for it to wake up once the conditions are right!

Hey there! So, it turns out that China has officially named its first Mars rover Zhurong, and the announcement was made by the China National Space Administration (CNSA) on April 24, which is also China’s Space Day. Pretty cool, right? Zhurong is actually the god of fire from ancient Chinese mythology, and it ties in nicely with the name for Mars in Chinese, which is Huoxing, meaning "the planet of fire." 

Fire has always been important for humanity—bringing warmth and light—and naming the rover after the god of fire is a way of showing that China is ready to spark its journey into planetary exploration. Wu Yanhua, the deputy director of CNSA, mentioned that "Zhu" translates to "wish," symbolizing our hopes for exploring the universe, while "Rong" means "integration and cooperation," highlighting China’s goal for peaceful space endeavors and building a community for the future of all humanity.

This name choice is just one example of how Chinese aerospace workers blend science with culture, just like they did with other spacecraft names like Tianwen, Chang’e, and Beidou. It really reflects the spirit of exploration and confidence in Chinese culture. And just so you know, China launched its Mars probe, Tianwen-1, back on July 23, 2020. It’s made up of an orbiter, a lander, and, of course, this rover that’s now officially named Zhurong, which entered Mars' orbit on February 24. Exciting stuff ahead!

Hey there! So, when you click on links from our site and make a purchase, we might earn a little commission—just wanted to give you a heads-up! Now, here’s something cool: China is running a 40-day public vote to pick a name for its Mars rover that’s getting closer to the Red Planet. You can actually vote for your favorite from a list of 10 names for the Tianwen-1 mission rover. The names are pretty interesting: Hongyi, Qilin, Nezha, Chitu, Zhurong, Qiusuo, Fenghuolun, Zhuimeng, Tianxing, and Xinghuo. They all draw inspiration from Chinese mythology, Confucian ideas, and legendary creatures. Oh, and check this out: Hongyi, which comes from the Confucian Analects, means "persistence" or perseverance, kinda like NASA's Perseverance rover that's also on its way to Mars! The Lunar Exploration and Space Engineering Center from the China National Space Administration (CNSA) shared this shortlist on January 18 after collecting suggestions since the mission kicked off last July. By the way, the Tianwen-1 mission includes both an orbiter and a rover, and they’re all set to orbit Mars around February 10. The rover won’t land until around May, though. Exciting stuff, right?

So, Zhurong is named after this cool figure from Chinese mythology who's all about fire and light. You know, Mars is often called "the Planet of Fire" in China and other East Asian countries. The name Zhurong was actually picked through a public online vote that ran from January 20 to February 28, 2021, and it got a whopping 504,466 votes to come in first place! The idea behind the name is pretty inspiring—it's meant to symbolize sparking interest in interstellar exploration in China and show the determination of the Chinese people to explore the stars and discover the unknowns in the universe. 

China's journey into interplanetary exploration started back in 2011 when they tried to send Yinghuo-1, a Mars orbiter, into space in a partnership with Russia. Unfortunately, it never made it past Earth's orbit because the Russian launch vehicle failed. But that didn't stop them! The China National Space Administration (CNSA) decided to go solo for their Mars mission. Fast forward to November 2014, and they even showcased an early model of their future Mars rover at the 10th China International Aviation & Aerospace Exhibition. It looked a lot like the Yutu lunar rover that had landed on the Moon. Then, on April 22, 2016, Xu Dazhe, who heads up CNSA, announced that the Mars mission got the green light on January 11, 2016.

So, on May 14, 2021, the lander and the Zhurong rover broke away from the Tianwen-1 orbiter. They had a pretty intense nine-minute entry into Mars’ atmosphere, but they nailed the landing in Utopia Planitia. They used a mix of an aeroshell, a parachute, and a retrorocket to pull it off. With this successful landing, China became the second country ever to have a fully operational spacecraft on the surface of Mars, right after the U.S. Then, on May 19, 2021, once they got a stable connection with the rover, the China National Space Administration (CNSA) shared the first images from Mars. Fast forward to May 22, 2021, at 10:10 a.m. Beijing time (that’s 02:40 GMT), Zhurong rolled off its landing platform and kicked off its exploration mission. By June 11, 2021, CNSA was back at it, releasing the first batch of scientific images from Mars. This included a stunning panoramic shot taken by Zhurong and a colorful group photo of both Zhurong and the Tianwen-1 lander, snapped by a wireless camera sitting on Martian soil. The panoramic image was made up of 24 individual shots captured by the Navigation and Topography Camera before the rover set out on its adventure.

You know, besides Zhurong, there are actually two other rovers cruising around Mars – NASA's Perseverance and Curiosity. Perseverance has been exploring the Martian surface for over two years now, while Curiosity has been doing its thing for about ten years!

So, on February 27, 2023, the first results from the meteorological data collected over the first 325 sols of the Tianwen-1 mission were published in the journal Nature. To meet its scientific goals, the Tianwen-1 orbiter is loaded up with eight scientific instruments, and the Zhurong rover has six of its own. By the way, the lander didn’t carry any scientific instruments, but it did have a Mars Emergency Beacon, which is pretty cool—it’s built to survive a major crash and could help gather important engineering data for future missions. Oh, and just like the orbiter, the lander also brought along the Chinese flag and the mascots from the 2022 Winter Olympics and Paralympics. Plus, Argentina's Comisión Nacional de Actividades Espaciales (CONAE) is teaming up on this project through the Espacio Lejano tracking station in Las Lajas, Neuquén. This station even helped with China’s Chang'e 4 mission on the Moon back in January 2019. And let’s not forget, France's Institut de Recherche en Astrophysique et Planétologie (IRAP) in Toulouse is also involved with the Zhurong rover.

Wow, can you believe it? After a historic landing on Mars, the Zhurong rover is all set to look for signs of water and maybe even life! Today was a huge day for China's space program, as they successfully landed the Zhurong rover on Mars, making them the second country ever to pull off such a feat. Now, teams are gearing up to roll the rover off its landing platform and onto the dusty Martian surface to kick off its mission. This is all happening in Utopia Planitia, a massive plain that might have once been home to an ancient Martian ocean. 

The Zhurong, which weighs about 529 pounds, had quite the adventure getting down to the surface. It went through a wild descent, slowing down from supersonic speeds with a parachute, and then using retrorockets to land safely. Fun fact: it’s named after an ancient Chinese fire god and is similar in size to NASA’s Spirit and Opportunity rovers, which landed back in 2004 and sent back some amazing images and data about Mars. Who knows what discoveries China’s rover might make about water and the potential for life there? This could really open doors for future human missions to Mars!

"Did you know that back in the day, China was all about ocean exploration? Zhang Xiaoping, an associate professor at the State Key Laboratory of Lunar and Planetary Sciences at the Macau University of Science and Technology, points out that Zheng He’s voyages to Southeast Asia and Africa in the early 15th century are a big part of that history. He sees China’s Mars mission as a modern-day extension of those Ming Dynasty adventures. According to him, missions like Tianwen-1 and the Zhurong rover are super important. They help us dive into the mysteries of the universe, spark excitement for science among young people, boost creativity across the country, and improve our ability to explore the unknown, all while expanding humanity’s horizons. Oh, and by the way, this story was updated to celebrate the successful landing of China’s Zhurong rover on Mars! Also, the image of China’s Chang'e-3 lander and rover has been adjusted a bit to take out a background shot of Earth that wasn’t part of the original mission photos."

So, let’s talk about Tianwen-1, or TW-1 for short. It’s this really cool interplanetary mission by the China National Space Administration (CNSA) that sent a robotic spacecraft over to Mars. The whole setup includes six different spacecraft: there’s an orbiter, two deployable cameras, a lander, a remote camera, and of course, the Zhurong rover. All of these together weigh almost five tons, making it one of the heaviest probes ever launched to Mars, plus it’s packed with 14 scientific instruments. This mission is just the first in a series that CNSA has planned as part of their Planetary Exploration of China program. 

What’s the mission all about? Well, they aim to investigate the geology and internal structure of Mars, look for signs of water—both past and present—and get a better understanding of the Martian atmosphere and space environment. They launched it from the Wenchang Spacecraft Launch Site on July 23, 2020, using a Long March 5 heavy-lift rocket. After a seven-month journey through the inner Solar System, the spacecraft finally reached Martian orbit on February 10, 2021. For the next three months, it took a good look at potential landing sites from its orbit. Pretty exciting stuff, right?

So, here’s the scoop: the Field Test Rover, or FTR for short, was actually built two years before they put together the Zhurong rover. Some of its parts even made their way into the vehicle that flew to Mars! Before the big mission kicked off, the FTR went through thousands of tests right here on Earth. It was like a practice buddy for the scientists and engineers, helping them map out the best path for Zhurong by testing out different maneuvers in what they called the Mars yard. 

Now, when it came to picking a landing spot, they had a couple of spots in mind: Chryse Planitia and Utopia Planitia. The team really liked Utopia Planitia because they thought it had a better shot at revealing whether there used to be an ancient ocean up north on Mars. In the end, that’s where they decided to land! 

On July 23, 2020, at 12:41 AM UTC+8, they launched Tianwen-1 along with the Zhurong rover from the Wenchang Spacecraft Launch Site using a Long March 5 heavy-lift rocket. After a 202-day journey through space, Tianwen-1 slipped into Martian orbit on February 10, 2021, making it China’s first Mars orbiter! Afterward, it did a few orbital maneuvers and got busy surveying potential landing sites on Mars to prep for the big landing.

So, the rover is about 1 meter tall and weighs around 240 kg (that's about 530 pounds). After it was deployed, the orbiter took on the role of a communications relay for the rover, all while still keeping an eye on Mars from orbit. Then, on July 12, 2021, Zhurong checked out the parachute and backshell that had landed on the Martian surface when it made its descent on May 14. Fast forward to August 15, 2021, and Zhurong wrapped up its planned exploration tasks but didn’t stop there; it kept driving towards the southern part of Utopia Planitia where it first landed. By August 18, 2021, Zhurong had already exceeded its expected lifespan of 90 sols, and the scientists in China announced they were extending its mission to explore an ancient coastal area on Mars. From mid-September to late October 2021, both the Tianwen-1 orbiter and Zhurong rover went into safe mode because of a communications blackout around solar conjunction, but they were back up and running once that was over. Then, on May 20, 2022, Zhurong was put into hibernation mode to get ready for some sandstorms and the Martian winter, and it was set to wake itself up when the temperature and sunlight were just right.

So, on June 11, 2021, CNSA shared some exciting news: they released the first batch of scientific images from Mars! This included a stunning panoramic shot taken by the Zhurong rover, along with a group photo featuring both Zhurong and the Tianwen-1 lander snapped by a drop camera. The panoramic image was actually made up of 24 individual shots captured by the NaTeCam before the rover made its way down to the Martian surface. It turns out that the landscape and rock distribution near where they landed matched what scientists expected based on previous studies of the south Utopia Planitia area, showing lots of small rocks, some neat white wave patterns, and even mud volcanoes.

Just a little earlier, on May 22, 2021, at 02:40 UTC, the Zhurong rover made its descent from the lander to kick off its scientific mission on Mars. The first pictures we got back on Earth after it landed showed the empty landing platform and the ramps that helped the rover descend. During this deployment, Zhurong's Mars Climatic Station recorded sounds, making it the second device ever to capture Martian sounds—right after the microphones on the Mars 2020 Perseverance rover. Plus, that drop camera we mentioned? It managed to take photos of both the Zhurong rover and the Tianwen-1 lander. The plan is for the Zhurong rover to explore the Martian surface for 90 sols, and it stands about 1.85 meters tall, which is around 6 feet.

So, after checking everything was good to go, the spacecraft kicked off its scientific work with the Mars Energetic Particle Analyzer, which is attached to the orbiter. It started sending back some initial data to ground control. Then, on May 14, 2021, the lander and rover duo made their attempt to land on Mars. About nine minutes after they entered the Martian atmosphere in their protective shell, the lander, carrying the rover, successfully landed in a spot called Utopia Planitia. After a bit of time spent running system checks and snapping some engineering selfies, the lander released the Zhurong rover to start its work on the surface. This little rover runs on solar power and is set to explore the Martian ground with radar, do some chemical tests on the soil, and even search for signs of life. This mission marks China’s first venture into interplanetary exploration and their first independent Mars probe. The main goal? To test out China's deep space communication and control tech and prove they can orbit and land spacecraft successfully. From a science perspective, the mission has five key objectives: it aims to look for signs of both current and past life, create surface maps, analyze soil composition and water ice distribution, and study the Martian atmosphere, especially the ionosphere.

So, Zhurong was supposed to wake up from its planned nap in December, but it looks like dust buildup might have messed with its power. China’s Mars rover, which has been snoozing on the red planet since May 2022, has probably collected a lot of sand and dust, according to its mission designer, who finally spoke up after months of silence. This fully robotic rover, named after a mythical Chinese fire god, was anticipated to come back online in December after entering sleep mode last May because the winter on Mars reduced solar power. It’s likely that all that dust got in the way of generating power and waking up, as reported by China’s state TV, CCTV, quoting Zhang Rongqiao, the chief designer of China’s Mars exploration program. The Zhurong has been exploring the Martian surface for an impressive 358 days and has traveled about 1,921 meters (or 2,100 yards), which is way beyond its original three-month mission. Images sent back from Mars show that the rover hasn’t moved at all between at least September 8, 2022, and February 7, 2023, according to the University of Arizona, which runs a high-resolution camera on NASA’s Mars Reconnaissance Orbiter—the most powerful camera ever sent to another planet.

"Landing safely on Mars is no small feat, especially since it was China’s first attempt at a soft landing,” said Long Xiao, a planetary scientist at the China University of Geosciences, just before everything came together. “But this is a crucial step for exploring Mars and deep space.” Getting down to the Martian surface is quite a challenge! Up until now, NASA was the only one who had successfully landed and operated spacecraft there; remember the Soviet Mars 3 lander from 1971? It managed to send back half a photo before going silent just 100 seconds into its mission. With the Zhurong rover landing and rolling around on Mars, China has really made a leap ahead of many other space-faring nations. The rover made it through the nerve-wracking “seven minutes of terror,” which is the time it takes from entering the atmosphere to actually landing. The China National Space Administration (CNSA) had to watch the whole autonomous landing from nearly 200 million miles away—so far that it takes 18 minutes just to send a signal back and forth! They were just hoping everything went smoothly. Zhurong was attached to its orbiter, safely tucked inside an aeroshell meant to protect it during the trip through Mars' atmosphere. Once it was released and survived the fiery entry, a big parachute deployed to slow it down even more. Finally, a landing platform with rocket engines kicked in for the last stretch to the surface.

So, here’s the scoop: a laser range finder and a 3D scanner helped gather altitude and terrain info, while cameras took care of picking the perfect landing spot all on their own. Michel Blanc from the Research Institute in Astrophysics and Planetology in France mentions that landing on Mars is way trickier than landing on the moon. But here’s the thing—China has had a string of successful missions to the moon that really set them up for a Mars landing. Take Chang’e-4, for example; it made history by landing on the far side of the moon in 2019, and that required some serious tech know-how in artificial intelligence and avoiding hazards, according to Blanc. Plus, the rocket engines on Zhurong are pretty similar to those that helped safely land three spacecraft on the moon. And let’s not forget about the supersonic parachutes China has used to bring astronauts back from Earth orbit in their capsules. All of this tech is what’s gearing up CNSA to nail that Martian landing. 

Once Zhurong’s six wheels hit the Martian dust after rolling off the landing platform, the rover will open up its cool, butterfly-like solar panels and start its main mission, which is set to last about three months. But hey, it could go way beyond that! Just look at the Spirit and Opportunity rovers—they were meant to last around 90 days, but they ended up exploring Mars for years. And Utopia Planitia, where Zhurong is headed, is believed to have once been an ancient sea, with sedimentary layers that might just hold clues about past water.

Did you know that NASA’s Viking 2 lander, which touched down just north of where Zhurong landed back in 1975, captured some pretty cool stuff? It spotted things like ice contractions and frost on Mars’ surface, plus some unique polygon patterns that might have formed due to changes in subsurface ice over the seasons. Now, Zhurong is equipped with ground-penetrating radar that will send out signals at two different frequencies to gather echo data from beneath the surface, reaching down about 33 feet to look for ice or salty water hidden underground. According to Head, “Tianwen-1 should be able to find any snow and ice lurking below the surface with its instruments.” These ice pockets could be super important for future crewed missions, and any water or brine that’s shielded from harsh radiation could actually support simple life forms. China is planning to share all the data from Tianwen-1 and Zhurong just like it has with its lunar missions, which is great news for planetary scientists everywhere. Plus, this mission is just the beginning for China; they’re gearing up for a bold sample-return mission to Mars that’s set to launch around 2028. And if that’s not enough, they’re also looking to send a probe to Jupiter, possibly land on its moon Callisto, scoop up samples from a near-Earth asteroid, and even launch a couple of Voyager-like spacecraft toward the far reaches of our solar system. Exciting times ahead!

So, this six-wheeled rover tips the scales at about 240 kg and stands 1.85 m tall. It gets its power from four solar panels and a special fuel called n-undecane, which is stored in 10 containers under two round windows on the deck. During the day, it soaks up heat and melts, then at night, it solidifies and releases that heat. It’s got six scientific instruments on board. Among them, RoPeR is busy while the rover is on the move, while MarSCoDe, MSCam, and NaTeCam do their thing when it's stationary. RoMAG and MCS can operate whether the rover is moving or still. The rover was designed to work for about 90 sols, with each three sols counting as one operation period. Here’s how it works: data gets sent down every sol, and then teams at CNSA process it over a 5 to 6 month period before sharing it with the scientific community. Oh, and in July 2021, the designer of the Tianwen-1 orbiter shared some exciting news: because Zhurong performed better than expected, they combined the original three-day operational periods into one, speeding up its exploration! During one of its traverses, it looked at five dunes, including a neat eroded barchan with bright sands making up its main body, topped off with some darker sands.

If you want to learn more about Wounded Warrior Project and how they’re helping warriors, their families, and caregivers achieve their goals, just check them out!

You know, Patricia Wright is quite the remarkable person! She's not just a renowned anthropologist and primatologist but also the Herrnstein Professor of Conservation Biology and a Distinguished Service Professor at Stony Brook University. Plus, she founded and runs the Centre ValBio research station in Madagascar. Recently, she was honored as the first-ever recipient of the Conservation Warrior Award, which was set up by the Seneca Park Zoo in Monroe County, New York, along with the Seneca Park Zoo Society. The award is all about recognizing people whose innovative ideas and dedication to conservation have really made a difference in helping species survive. It also aims to shine a light on the amazing work being done globally in partnership with the zoo. The award itself is pretty cool—it’s a sculpture of a tree growing out of a rock, created by artists at the Corning Museum of Glass, symbolizing the strength and passion that Patricia brings to wildlife preservation. On top of that, she received a generous unrestricted grant of $20,000, thanks to Claudia and David Weber. Oh, and on December 7, 2022, Monroe County Executive Adam Bello even declared it Dr. Patricia Wright Day! He encouraged everyone to keep pushing for conservation efforts both locally and around the world while celebrating the incredible contributions of Dr. Wright. Zoo Society President and CEO Pamela Reed Sanchez also emphasized how important it is to have conservation heroes, especially with all the recent news in mind.

query: Who stars in The Lost City?
positive: So, have you heard about "The Lost City"? It stars Sandra Bullock, Channing Tatum, and Daniel Radcliffe, along with a few other talented folks. There are some pretty fun facts about the cast, too! This 2022 film is a fantastic mix of comedy, adventure, romance, and an amazing cast that really nails their roles. Directed by Adam and Aaron Nee, the story comes from Seth Gordon and revolves around Loretta Sage, a brilliant author who writes romance novels set in exotic places, featuring characters like Dr. Angela Lovemore and her love interest, Dash McMahon. But here’s the twist: Loretta's real life is nothing like the stories she writes. After her husband's passing, she turns into a bit of a recluse and isn’t even interested in writing anymore. During a promotional tour for her latest book, she gets kidnapped by a crazy billionaire who wants her to help him find a treasure from her book. Luckily, she's not alone in this mess—Alan, the cover model for her novels, decides to step up and rescue her, proving he can be a hero too. Alongside Sandra Bullock and Channing Tatum, the film features Daniel Radcliffe, Da'Vine Joy Randolph, Brad Pitt, and Oscar Nunez, among others. "The Lost City" hit theaters in March 2022 and quickly became a box office sensation!

Hey there! Get excited because The Lost City, one of the most talked-about movies this year, is hitting theaters later this week! Finally, all you movie lovers will be able to catch this flick, which is rumored to be one of the most action-packed releases of 2022. Just from the trailers with all those explosions and fight scenes, it looks like the buzz is totally spot on. Plus, we’ve heard that the story is super engaging, so you definitely won’t want to miss it. Want to know what it’s all about? Here’s a sneak peek at the synopsis for The Lost City. 

And the cast? Oh man, it’s just as exciting as the plot! Who can you expect to see? Well, we’ve got Channing Tatum, the Dog actor, playing Alan, and Daniel Radcliffe from Miracle Workers as Fairfax. And let’s not forget the fabulous Sandra Bullock, who’s taking on the lead role of Loretta Sage. Check out the official trailer for The Lost City below to see Bullock and the rest of this amazing cast in action!

So, when Adam and Aaron Nee were gearing up to make their film The Lost City, they really took their actors on a wild ride. Adam recalls a moment with Sandra Bullock and Channing Tatum where they had them in the ocean, surrounded by jellyfish, and he thought, “Oh no, we might lose them right at the start!” The movie follows Sandra's character, Loretta, a successful romance novelist who's hit a creative wall. During her latest book tour with her dashing cover model, Alan (played by Channing), they end up in a pretty intense kidnapping scenario that leads to a wild jungle chase. While chatting with the cast—Sandra, Daniel Radcliffe, Da'Vine Joy Randolph, Oscar Nuñez, and Patti Harrison—during EW’s Around the Table, the directors described filming in the Dominican Republic's jungles as “a crazy, zany time.” And it wasn’t just Sandra and Channing they were worried about scaring off. Aaron remembers how they felt when Daniel, playing the quirky billionaire villain Abigail Fairfax, first showed up on set. He joked, “I think Adam and I were like, ‘Oh no, we just got Daniel here, and now we’re throwing him in the ocean!’” To which Sandra chimed in, “And now, we’re going to kill him!”

You know, despite all the concerns about things going wrong, the Nee brothers feel like “a lot went according to plan.” Aaron mentioned, “We set a goal and made it happen, and everyone really stepped up. But that’s the cool thing about film as an art form; it’s a team effort. You bring in talented people who excel at their craft, and the project kind of takes on a life of its own because it’s not just one person doing everything.” If you want to hear more, check out the full roundtable with the cast and directors of The Lost City above, and don’t forget to catch the film in theaters starting Friday. Oh, and by the way, Sandra Bullock and Channing Tatum are joining forces for an action-adventure rom-com that hits theaters on April 15, 2022!

So, "The Lost City" is this fun action-adventure comedy from 2022, directed by the Nee brothers. They teamed up with Oren Uziel and Dana Fox to write the screenplay, all based on a story by Seth Gordon. It features a star-studded cast, including Sandra Bullock, Channing Tatum, Daniel Radcliffe, Da'Vine Joy Randolph, and even Brad Pitt! The story is about a romance novelist and her cover model who find themselves on the run from a billionaire who wants her to help him locate an ancient burial chamber mentioned in one of her books. They first announced the project back in October 2020, with Bullock coming on board as both producer and lead actress, and Tatum signing up in December. The rest of the cast came together the following year. They filmed in the beautiful Dominican Republic from May to August 2021. The film made its debut at South by Southwest on March 12, 2022, and hit theaters in the U.S. on March 25, 2022, courtesy of Paramount Pictures. Critics generally liked it, and it ended up making over $190 million worldwide, which is pretty impressive considering its $68 million budget. The main character, Loretta Sage, writes these romance-adventure novels featuring a fictional heroine named Dr. Angela Lovemore and her love interest, Dash McMahon.

You know, "The Lost City" really hit it out of the park with its reviews, and a big part of that is thanks to the amazing chemistry between the actors, their performances, and the way they brought this adventure story to life. Even though it doesn’t break new ground, it still feels fresh and super entertaining! Speaking of the cast, let's dive into some fun facts about them. Sandra Bullock? She’s a total Hollywood icon and one of the highest-paid actresses around the globe. Over the years, she’s amazed us with her incredible range in all sorts of films, snagging a bunch of awards along the way. She became a household name with hits like "Speed," "Miss Congeniality," and "While You Were Sleeping," just to name a few. But acting isn’t the only thing she’s talented at—she’s got a whole bunch of skills up her sleeve! Did you know she’s a pretty good piano player? She took lessons for 12 years, although she didn’t really love it. On top of that, she speaks German and has also taken ballet and vocal arts classes! And here’s a fun tidbit: Channing Tatum, who kicked off his career in 2004, didn’t really become a household name until 2012 when he played a male stripper in "Magic Mike."

So, their comedy movie is really going to be watched closely at the box office. But you know what? They’re totally unfazed by it and have a lot to say about that Brad Pitt cameo, “Magic Mike,” and “Harry Potter.” Channing Tatum, Sandra Bullock, and Daniel Radcliffe are all starring in this rom-com adventure called “The Lost City.” 

On a fun Zoom call with Daniel, Sandra and Channing shared a bit about their off-screen friendship. It turns out they’re not just co-stars; they’re also play-date parents! Their daughters, Laila and Everly, are at that age where sleepovers are a thing now. Sandra joked with Daniel, saying, “You’ll get to experience this one day. It’s such a big deal when you can finally let your kid go to someone else’s house without worrying they’ll be in danger.” 

In “The Lost City,” hitting theaters March 25, Sandra plays Loretta Sage, a romance novelist whose life is pretty routine until a quirky rich guy, played by Radcliffe, kidnaps her, thinking she can help him find some hidden treasure in the jungle.

So, let’s talk about Channing Tatum. He’s basically the King of the Often Dancing Himbos, right? I mean, just look at his resume with hits like Step Up, Magic Mike, and 21 Jump Street. In Hollywood, it’s all about using those big names wisely—think Bullock, Pitt, Radcliffe, and of course, Tatum. And honestly, the casting in The Lost City is pretty spot-on. You can catch it streaming right now on Paramount+. Seriously, the roles are just perfect! 

In The Lost City, you’ve got Sandra Bullock as Loretta Sage, a widowed romance novelist who writes about this super well-researched lost city. Her books feature a fictional hunk named Dash, and guess who’s playing him? Yep, it’s Channing Tatum as Alan, the model who brings Dash to life—wig and frilly shirt included! He might not be the sharpest tool in the shed, but he’s got a heart of gold and really wants Loretta to see him as more than just a pretty face. It’s like a classic case of brilliant lady novelist meets Himbo Prime. You can probably guess where this is heading. But wait, it gets crazier! There’s this wild billionaire named Abigail Spencer, played by Dan Radcliffe, who kidnaps Loretta and tries to win her over with “all of the cheeses” to help him find that actual lost city from her books. Wild, right?

So, there’s this mid-credits scene where Loretta and Alan find themselves at a meditation class, and guess what? They run into Jack, who somehow survived a gunshot to the head! He claims he did it by using a “different” ten percent of his brain—pretty wild, right? Oh, and we also get to see Raymond Lee as Officer Gomez, Adam Nee as Officer Sawyer, and Stephen Lang, who’s hilariously credited as Slang, playing the fantasy villain that Loretta imagines. 

Now, back in October 2020, it was announced that Sandra Bullock would star in this film called The Lost City of D. The Nee brothers are directing, with a screenplay by Seth Gordon and Dana Fox, and Sandra’s also producing it under her Fortis Films label, while Paramount Pictures is handling distribution. Interestingly, Bullock initially passed on the project because she thought the story felt “outdated” after being in development for seven years! But then in December, Channing Tatum came on board as the male lead. 

Between March and April 2021, they added more cast members like Patti Harrison, Da'Vine Joy Randolph, Daniel Radcliffe, Brad Pitt, and Oscar Nunez, with Pitt and Lang popping up in cameo roles. Principal photography kicked off in May 2021 in the Dominican Republic, shooting in places like Samaná, Santo Domingo, Casa de Campo, Monte Plata Province, and Pinewood Dominican Republic Studios.

query: What happened at the Academy Awards involving Will Smith and Chris Rock?
positive: So, here’s the scoop: The Oscars telecast cut out some pretty intense moments from that Will Smith and Chris Rock showdown. According to the New York Times, Will Smith is now banned from the Oscars for a decade after he slapped Chris Rock right on camera during the show. This all went down in the last hour of what was otherwise a pretty dull Oscars on March 27. Rock made a joke about Jada Pinkett Smith, Will’s wife, and that’s when Will jumped on stage and slapped him. Just minutes later, he actually won his very first Oscar! Diddy couldn’t believe it and said, “I did not know this was gonna be the most exciting Oscars ever.” The Academy of Motion Picture Arts and Sciences later addressed the situation in an open letter on April 8, saying, “This action we are taking today in response to Will Smith’s behavior is a step toward a larger goal of protecting the safety of our performers and guests, and restoring trust in the Academy.” They also mentioned hoping for a time of healing for everyone involved. Will, who stepped down from the Academy on April 1, said, “I accept and respect the Academy’s decision.” So, what actually happened? Well, Chris Rock was there to announce the winner for Best Documentary, and before he got to the nominees, he was doing his usual comedic bit, and then he made a comment about Jada, who was sitting right in front of him in a green gown with a shaved head.

You remember that crazy moment at the Oscars, right? It’s the one everyone’s been calling “the slap.” Will Smith actually hit comedian Chris Rock onstage at the 2022 Oscars after Rock made a joke about Jada Pinkett Smith, Will's wife. Since then, a ton has gone down: Will apologized not once but twice and got banned from Academy events for ten years. Chris Rock finally talked about the whole thing in detail during a live comedy special, almost a year after it all happened. And guess what? The 2023 Oscars went by without any slaps, but you can bet that moment was still a hot topic during the show. So, here’s the scoop: the drama kicked off when Rock took the stage to present the award for best documentary feature. He made a joke about a possible "G.I. Jane" sequel because of Jada’s bald head. Will initially laughed at the joke, but Jada didn’t look happy at all. Then, out of nowhere, Will walked up, slapped Rock, and went back to his seat, leaving everyone in the audience confused about whether it was all part of the show. And Rock just stood there, saying, “Will Smith just slapped the (expletive) out of me,” totally deadpan.

So, at the 94th Academy Awards on March 27, 2022, something pretty shocking happened: Will Smith walked right up on stage and slapped Chris Rock while he was presenting the Best Documentary Feature award. This all went down because Rock made a joke about Jada Pinkett Smith's shaved head, which she's been rocking since 2021 because of alopecia. After the slap, Will went back to his seat and yelled some pretty intense stuff at Rock, who managed to finish his presentation without any more interruptions. Later that night, Will snagged the Best Actor award and apologized to the Academy and all the other nominees in his speech, but he didn’t say sorry to Rock at that moment. The next day, though, he took to social media to apologize to both Rock and the Academy. Then, on April 1, he decided to resign from the Academy, likely to avoid a suspension or being kicked out, and just a week later, he got banned from attending any Academy events for ten years. Most live broadcasts in the U.S. muted what happened during the incident because of censorship laws, but some uncensored footage from international broadcasts blew up on social media, especially a clip from the Australian coverage that became super popular in the first 24 hours. This whole thing got a ton of attention and pretty much overshadowed everything else that happened that night. Afterward, some media outlets reminded everyone of Rock’s earlier comments about Jada from 1997, when he talked about her being at the Million Woman March on his late-night show.

Hey there! So, it's been nearly a year since Will Smith slapped Chris Rock at the Oscars, and guess what? Rock finally took a crack at it during a live Netflix show on Saturday night. He spent the last few minutes poking fun at Smith and his wife, Jada Pinkett Smith. He even joked, “Now I watch ‘Emancipation,’ just to see him get whooped,” referring to that Civil War movie where Smith plays an enslaved guy. After the slap, everyone was waiting for Rock to spill his thoughts since he’s not one to shy away from a bit of controversy. But instead, he played it cool at his first show after the incident, saying he was still processing everything and promised he’d talk about it when the time was right. Well, that time came Saturday at the Hippodrome Theater in Baltimore, where his stand-up set was live-streamed for everyone to see. At one point, he even made fun of himself, joking about how much bigger Smith is. He said, “Will Smith does movies with his shirt off,” while strutting around the stage in his white outfit. And he quipped, “You never seen me do a movie with my shirt off.” Classic Rock!

“Hey, Jada, I love you,” Rock said. “Can’t wait to see G.I. Jane 2, all right?” But then Jada’s face went completely stone-faced, and out of nowhere, Smith jumped up, walked right up to the stage, and slapped Rock! The US broadcast cut the audio right then, but other countries, like Japan and Australia, got the uncensored version. “Wow! Wow!” Rock exclaimed, swaying a bit as Smith marched back to his seat. “Will Smith just smacked the crap out of me, everyone.” Then Smith yelled from the audience, “Keep my wife’s name out your fucking mouth!” Rock replied, “Wow, dude, it was just a G.I. Jane joke.” Smith shot back, “Keep my wife’s name out your fucking mouth!” Rock said, “I’m going to. Okay?” He paused for a sec, looked a bit shocked, and just as the US audio kicked back in, he said, “That was the greatest night in the history of television. Okay. Okay.” Then he carried on with presenting the Oscar, which went to Summer of Soul, directed by Questlove. Just a little while later, Smith snagged the Oscar for Best Actor for his role in King Richard, where he played Richard Williams, the dad of Venus and Serena Williams. “Richard Williams was a fierce defender of his family,” Smith started as he took the stage.

Meanwhile, the Academy of Motion Picture Arts and Sciences decided to ban Will Smith from all their events for the next ten years. They made it clear in a statement that from April 8, 2022, he can't attend anything, whether in person or online, including the Oscars. query: When did Apple M2 chip?
positive: So, the Apple M2 is this cool series of ARM-based chips that Apple designed for their Mac desktops, notebooks, and the iPad Pro. It's actually the second generation of their ARM architecture since they switched from Intel Core to their own Apple silicon, following the M1. Apple rolled out the M2 on June 6, 2022, at WWDC, and they also unveiled new models of the MacBook Air and the 13-inch MacBook Pro that use it. This chip is built using TSMC's "Enhanced 5-nanometer technology," and it packs a whopping 20 billion transistors, which is a 25% jump from the M1. Apple says you can expect CPU performance boosts of up to 18% and GPU gains of up to 35% compared to the M1. Then, in January 2023, they introduced the M2 Pro and M2 Max chips for professionals. The M2 Max is a beefier version of the M2 Pro, offering more GPU cores and memory bandwidth, plus a bigger die size. And just recently, in June 2023, Apple launched the M2 Ultra, which combines two M2 Max chips into one package.

This is pretty exciting because the M2 is basically an upgrade from the fantastic M1 chip that hit the scene back in 2020. It's got the power behind Apple's new 13-inch MacBook Air and MacBook Pro for 2022. These models are the successors to some of the top MacBooks out there, so it’ll be interesting to see what the M2 can really do. You can find the M2 chip in two fresh laptops: the 13-inch MacBook Air and MacBook Pro, both of which launched in the summer of 2022. If you’re looking to snag one of these M2-powered MacBooks, be prepared to spend over a grand—$1,199 for the MacBook Air and $1,299 for the MacBook Pro. There’s also the Mac mini M2, which is a more affordable yet powerful little option from Apple. Plus, the M2 chip comes with an 8-core CPU and can be configured with up to a 10-core GPU!

So, back in June 2022, Apple introduced the M2 chip, their latest silicon marvel that follows up on the M1. If you're curious about the M2, this guide covers all the cool stuff you need to know, from how it boosts performance to the new features it brings along. 

The M2 is essentially Apple's next-gen System on a Chip (SoC) designed for Macs and iPads. It's part of Apple's journey to move away from Intel chips, which were the go-to for Macs until 2020. What’s neat about the M2 being a "System on a Chip" is that it combines several key components all in one place—think CPU, GPU, unified memory architecture (RAM), Neural Engine, Secure Enclave, SSD controller, image signal processor, and even Thunderbolt support with USB 4. This integration is what makes the M2 faster and more efficient compared to the old Intel chips. 

One of the standout features is the unified memory architecture. This means all the parts of the M2 can access the same data without jumping between different memory pools, which is a game-changer. The CPU, GPU, and other components can share data seamlessly, making everything run smoother and quicker.

Now, when it came to eight specific awards—like Best Animated Short Film and Best Original Score—they didn’t broadcast those live. Instead, they pre-taped them just an hour before the show started. The idea was to make room for more comedy, film clips, and music, and to keep the ceremony shorter. They had tried something similar at the 91st Academy Awards back in 2019, but it didn’t go over well, so they scrapped it. Rumor has it that ABC executives were pushing for this change, suggesting that if they didn’t move 12 categories off the live broadcast, they might not even air the ceremony. Josh Brolin and Jason Momoa ended up presenting those awards off-air, and people didn’t take that news too well. There was quite a bit of backlash!

Packer shared with Variety that "nobody is going to be shortchanged by this choice to make the show more efficient and speedy, and to get more folks tuning in." Fans could cast their votes using the #OscarsFanFavorite hashtag on Twitter. Don’t forget, the Oscars air this Sunday on ABC at 8 p.m. ET. You can keep up with everything on our live blog! They want him to finish his research, but there’s a twist—his lab will be on Parallaxis, the first luxury space station for billionaires. Rebecca Scherm is such a gem of a writer. So, back in 1889, Jules Verne decided to write a second sequel to his novel, The Purchase of the North Pole. In this one, the Gun Club members, led by J. T. Maston, come up with this wild plan to use the Columbiad to change the Earth's tilt. Why? To make it easier to tap into the mineral riches of the Arctic! Fast forward to March 1953, and the Gilberton Company released a comic-book version of From the Earth to the Moon as issue No. 105 in their Classics Illustrated series. A scriptwriter mixed Verne's original story with its sequel, Around the Moon. The talented Alex A. Blum, who was the art director, did both the cover art and the 44 pages inside. This title was really popular, going through twelve printings between 1953 and 1971. And here's a cool tidbit: during Apollo 11's return trip from the Moon, the crew actually mentioned Verne's book during a TV broadcast on July 23. Neil Armstrong, the mission's commander, said something like, "A hundred years ago, Jules Verne wrote a book about a voyage to the Moon. His spaceship, Columbia, took off from Florida and landed in the Pacific Ocean after completing a trip to the Moon. It seems fitting for us to share some of our thoughts as the modern-day Columbia gets ready to meet up with Earth and the same Pacific Ocean tomorrow." How neat is that?

Hey there! So, let’s talk about this interesting book called “A House Between the Earth and the Moon” by Rebecca Scherm. It’s her second novel, and it’s all about this fancy space station called Parallaxis, which is basically like a luxury condo in orbit for the super-rich, created by a tech company named Sensus. They’ve got a crew of top-notch scientists working on making this place livable for the long haul. The catch? Well, they don’t just get to live there—they're also part of a secret experiment. Sensus is using the station to test out this new surveillance and behavior modification system called Views. 

The story really captures the fear of what life might be like on Earth in the 2030s, where crazy weather events are so frequent that entire cities can just go up in flames, and even wealthy folks are constantly on the move to stay ahead of disasters. It’s a grim reality where mass casualties are just another headline, and privacy is pretty much non-existent unless you decide to go off the grid and miss out on alerts for floods or wildfires. You really feel for Alex, who’s working on this algae that can extract carbon. He’s in a race against time, wanting to save the planet, but with every passing year, it feels like his task becomes more urgent and way harder.

You know, it’s kind of strange how the Son sisters are mainly seen as just regular folks dealing with everyday issues, even while they’re deep into their mass mind-control project, Views. Then there’s Tess, a lonely data scientist who gets to peek into the lives of Parallaxis employees and their families through these visual feeds. She gets so wrapped up in watching them that it turns into a bit of stalking! It’s impressive how Scherm makes us care about these characters, even though they’re not exactly the most sympathetic. But honestly, she doesn’t really explain how Views is supposed to work or why it makes sense to have Tess monitor people in such a weird way, like watching them go to the bathroom. And that kind of issue pops up again at the end of the book, which gets really focused on personal feelings but doesn’t give us a solid conclusion to the bigger political and environmental problems. Still, overall, “A House Between the Earth and the Moon” is a really engaging read. Scherm does a fantastic job blending big tech themes with climate change, creating a world that captures the anxieties we’re all feeling these days. By the way, Sandra Newman, who wrote several novels including “The Heavens” and the upcoming “The Men,” is worth checking out! A HOUSE BETWEEN THE EARTH AND THE MOON by Rebecca Scherm is 400 pages and published by Viking for $27.

You know, Scherm’s writing isn’t exactly what you’d call stylish—there’s this moment where Mary Agnes is described as curled up “like a shrimp” that honestly made me cringe a bit—but it’s solid enough to take the story pretty far, and it moves along quickly. A House Between Earth and the Moon, which just came out this week, mixes up genres to create something fresh. It’s part family drama, part coming-of-age tale, part social commentary, and part cli-fi, and it’s original and touching, not in spite of its blend of big ideas, but because Scherm skillfully ties them all together. You could almost call it Everything Happens So Much. Mary Agnes, the youngest character we see things through, is really the heart of the story. She’s a lonely nerd who gets a thrill when her dad’s space adventures catch the eye of some popular kids at school. But even in the future, the in-crowd isn’t great, and her adorable crush has a bit of a sadistic side, thanks to technology. Those screen-obsessed, empathy-lacking teens remind me of the awkward bunch from M.T. Anderson’s Feed, and Mary Agnes’ journey feels like a really insightful young-adult novella woven throughout the book. Unfortunately, her little brother Shane doesn’t get as interesting of a storyline; his main trait seems to be that he’s “allergic to everything.”

Mary Agnes is really worried that her not-so-cool dad will get all caught up in the fancy Parallaxis I she sees in the promotional videos, but she’s got it all wrong. Sure, the ads make it look pristine, but in reality, it’s barely working. The Pioneers are having to play the role of DIY space-station builders just to get things up and running. Alex describes it as a “freezing, comfortless warehouse.” Imagine a zero-gravity version of those so-called “luxury” skyscrapers that are actually a bit of a mess, like 432 Park, where the high price and exclusivity hide all the flaws. Doja Cat was also in the mix with eight nominations and snagged a win for Best Pop Duo with her catchy track "Kiss Me More." And let’s not forget about Silk Sonic—they had four nominations and totally dominated the night, winning both Record of the Year and Song of the Year. So, the annual awards show is happening in Las Vegas this year, finally kicking off after being postponed because of the COVID-19 pandemic. The Grammys 2022 went down tonight (April 3) in Las Vegas, and it was a big night for folks like Jon Batiste, Silk Sonic, and Olivia Rodrigo. Most of the awards were handed out during the pre-telecast ceremony, which you could catch on the Grammys website and YouTube. Some early winners included Kanye West, who snagged Best Melodic Rap Performance for 'Hurricane' and Best Rap Song for 'Jail,' along with Tyler, The Creator winning Best Rap Album for 'Call Me If You Get Lost,' and Olivia Rodrigo taking home Best Pop Solo Performance for 'Drivers License.' Then, at 8 PM EST (1 AM BST), the main ceremony kicked off, and that's when they started handing out the biggest awards like Record Of The Year, Song Of The Year, Album Of The Year, and Best New Artist. Jon Batiste was the one to watch going into the show with 11 nominations, followed closely by Justin Bieber with eight, and Doja Cat, Rodrigo, and Billie Eilish each with seven nods. And guess what? Jon Batiste ended up being the biggest winner of the night, walking away with five awards, including Album Of The Year for 'We Are.' Silk Sonic also had a fantastic night, taking home four awards for their hit 'Leave The Door Open,' including Record Of The Year and Song Of The Year.

So, Batiste was up against some heavy hitters for Album Of The Year, like Lil Nas X's Montero, Olivia Rodrigo's SOUR, Taylor Swift's evermore, and Kanye West's Donda. He mentioned he’s had these "out of body experiences" with all of them, which is pretty cool. He said, "This is for real artists, real musicians. Let's just keep going," and then he wrapped things up with a motivating message: "Be you, that's it." Before snagging the Album Of The Year award, he also picked up gramophones for Best Music Video with "Freedom," Best American Roots Performance, and Best American Roots Song for "Cry" from his WE ARE album, plus Best Score Soundtrack For Visual Media for Soul. He was the most nominated artist that night with 11 nods! If you're curious, you can find the full list of winners and nominees from the 2022 GRAMMYs. And if you’re into rock 'n' roll, K-pop, R&B, or blues, August has some really great stuff coming your way!

This debut album from the rising star was all the rage. But guess what? It wasn’t Sour, or even the popular albums from other heavyweights like Taylor Swift, Billie Eilish, Lil Nas X, or Doja Cat, that snagged the top prize. So, the 64th Annual Grammy Awards took place at the MGM Grand Garden Arena in Las Vegas on April 3, 2022. This event was all about celebrating the best music from the eligibility year, which ran from September 1, 2020, to September 30, 2021. The nominations were shared during a virtual livestream on November 23, 2021, and the lineup of performers was announced on March 15, 24, and 30, 2022. Trevor Noah, who hosted the previous year’s ceremony, was back at the helm again. It was a big deal because this marked the first time the Recording Academy switched up host cities for just one ceremony. Plus, it was the first time the Grammys weren’t held in New York City or Los Angeles since 1973 when they took place at the Tennessee Theatre in Nashville. Jon Batiste led the pack with eleven nominations, while Doja Cat, H.E.R., and Justin Bieber each had eight. Batiste ended up snagging the most awards, winning five, including Album of the Year for his album "We Are." Silk Sonic grabbed both Record of the Year and Song of the Year for "Leave the Door Open," and Olivia Rodrigo was named Best New Artist. Oh, and it’s worth mentioning that the ceremony was originally supposed to happen on January 31, 2022, at the Crypto.
