negative: Hey there! Want to be in the loop about GRAMMY nominees, winners, and all the important happenings? You’re in the right place! So, who took home the awards in each of the 86 categories at the 2022 GRAMMYs? Just a heads up, the 2022 GRAMMYs Awards show, which is the 64th GRAMMY Awards, got moved to Sunday, April 3, at the MGM Grand Garden Arena in Las Vegas. We updated this info on Tuesday, Jan. 18, to let you know about the new date and place. Fast forward to Sunday, April 3, and the 2022 GRAMMYs are officially in the books! Curious about who snagged those golden gramophones? Check out the winners below! (Just to remind you, the 64th GRAMMY Awards celebrate recordings that came out between September 1, 2020, and September 30, 2021.) And don’t miss out on reliving the 10 must-see moments from the show! By the way, the Record of the Year award goes to the artist, plus the producer(s), recording engineer(s), mixer(s), and mastering engineer(s), if they’re not the artist.

So, Rodrigo really cleaned up at the awards, snagging three trophies, including Best New Artist. Kanye West and the duo of Tony Bennett and Lady Gaga each took home two awards as well. There was a heartfelt tribute to Taylor Hawkins, the Foo Fighters’ drummer, who sadly passed away just last week on March 25. The Foo Fighters were supposed to perform at the event, but they ended up pulling out and also canceled their tour plans. BTS wowed everyone with a fantastic performance of their hit ‘Butter’, taking on the role of undercover agents for the show. Billie Eilish brought the house down with ‘Happier Than Ever’, performing from a rain-soaked rooftop while wearing a t-shirt honoring Taylor Hawkins. Rodrigo also gave a stunning rendition of her breakthrough song ‘Drivers License’, and Lil Nas X teamed up with Jack Harlow for ‘Industry Baby’. Nas took us down memory lane with some classics, and Lady Gaga paid her respects to Bennett. If you’re curious about the full list of Grammy winners from 2022, here it is: ABBA – ‘I Still Have Faith In You’, Jon Batiste – ‘Freedom’, Tony Bennett & Lady Gaga – ‘I Get A Kick Out of You’, Justin Bieber, Daniel Caesar, Giveon – ‘Peaches’, Brandi Carlile – ‘Right on Time’, Doja Cat, SZA – ‘Kiss Me More’, Billie Eilish – ‘Happier Than Ever’, Lil Nas X – ‘Montero (Call Me By Your Name)’, and Olivia Rodrigo – ‘Drivers License’.

Jon Batiste is all smiles as he poses with four of his five shiny trophies at the 64th annual Grammy Awards. The event, held on April 3, 2022, in Las Vegas, was a big deal, especially since it was originally set for Los Angeles on January 31 but got pushed back because of COVID concerns. Below, you’ll find the complete list of nominees and winners (highlighted in bold) for the night: 

1. Record Of The Year  
2. Album Of The Year  
3. Song Of The Year  
4. Best New Artist  
5. Best Pop Solo Performance  
6. Best Pop Duo/Group Performance  
7. Best Traditional Pop Vocal Album  
8. Best Pop Vocal Album  
9. Best Dance/Electronic Recording  
10. Best Dance/Electronic Music Album  
11. Best Contemporary Instrumental Album  
12. Best Rock Performance  
13. Best Metal Performance  
14. Best Rock Song  
15. Best Rock Album  
16. Best Alternative Music Album  
17. Best R&B Performance (tie)  
18. Best Traditional R&B Performance  
19. Best R&B Song  
20. Best Progressive R&B Album  
21. Best R&B Album  
22. Best Rap Performance

So, for the 2022 ceremony, the Recording Academy decided to shake things up a bit. They cut down the number of categories that members could vote in to just ten, plus the four big ones. Those ten could come from up to three different fields, including the genres. The idea behind this change was to make sure the voting quality stayed high. 

Now, Drake was initially in the running for Best Rap Performance with "Way 2 Sexy" and Best Rap Album for Certified Lover Boy. But then, on December 6, 2021, he pulled out of both nominations, and the Academy respected his wishes and officially took him off the list. 

The ceremony was supposed to happen on January 31, 2022, at the Crypto.com Arena in Los Angeles. But on January 5, 2022, they postponed it indefinitely because of health and safety worries over the COVID-19 Omicron variant. With the venue booked solid for sports and concerts nearly every night until mid-April, they ended up moving the ceremony to the MGM Grand Garden Arena in Las Vegas. Fun fact: that arena hosted the Latin Grammy Awards for six years, including the 22nd Annual Latin Grammy Awards in November 2021. Finally, they announced the performers for the ceremony on March 25, 2022.

Hey there! So, let's talk about the nominees for the Best Immersive Audio Album at the 63rd GRAMMY Awards. We've got Alicia, who took home the win with some amazing work from George Massenburg and Eric Schilling on the mixing side, Michael Romanowski handling the mastering, and Ann Mincieli as the producer for Alicia Keys. Then there's Patricia Barber's "Clique," which features Jim Anderson and Ulrike Schwarz as mix engineers, with Bob Ludwig mastering and Jim Anderson producing. For Harry Styles’ "Fine Line," Greg Penny was the go-to guy for mixing, mastering, and producing—talk about multitasking! And we can't forget about "The Future Bites" by Steven Wilson, with Jake Fields and Steven Wilson mixing, and Dave Kosten also producing. Lastly, there's "Stille Grender," where Morten Lindberg took the lead on mixing, mastering, and producing for Anne Karin Sundal-Ask and Det Norske Jentekor. 

Oh, and a quick note—the meeting for the Best Immersive Audio Album Craft Committee had to be pushed back because of the COVID-19 pandemic, so they met after last year's GRAMMYs to go over everything. Now the nominations for the 63rd GRAMMYs are being voted on, and the winner will be announced during the 64th GRAMMY Awards. Exciting stuff!

So, let’s talk about the Best Regional Mexican Music Album (Including Tejano) category! This year, 2021 winner Natalia Lafourcade is back in the mix with her album *Un Canto por México - El Musical*, but she’s got some tough competition. She’s up against Chiquis with *Abeja Reina*, Los Tigres Del Norte’s *La Reunión (Deluxe)*, Christian Nodal’s EP *#1 Forajido*, and Marco Antonio Solís’ *Qué Ganas de Verte (Deluxe)*. 

Now, if we switch gears to the Best Tropical Latin Album, we’ve got Marc Anthony returning for another shot with his album *Pa'lla Voy*. He’s a two-time winner in this category, but he’s not alone! He’s competing against legends like Tito Nieves with *Legendario*, La Santa Cecilia’s *Quiero Verte Feliz*, Víctor Manuelle’s *Lado A Lado B*, Spanish Harlem Orchestra’s *Imágenes Latinas*, and Carlos Vives with *Cumbiana II*. 

If you want to check out all these amazing albums, there’s a fantastic playlist with 338 songs featuring all the Latin music GRAMMY nominees over on Pandora, Spotify, Apple Music, and Amazon Music. Don’t miss out, and we’ll catch you at Music's Biggest Night on Sunday, February 5!

Check out the latest videos, charts, and news! So, how did an album that didn’t exactly blow everyone away commercially or critically end up standing tall against some of the biggest albums of 2021? Let’s dive into five reasons that might help clear it up. 

First up, nomination momentum. It’s a bit of a meta situation, but you know how it goes—sometimes when one person gets recognized, it leads to more recognition, especially at award shows. And guess who was the most recognized artist going into this year’s Grammys? That’s right, Jon Batiste! The guy snagged an impressive 11 nominations, including a spot in the Big Four for Song of the Year with “Freedom,” plus nods in categories like Best R&B Performance, Best Improvised Jazz Solo, and Best American Roots Song. With that many nominations—three more than anyone else—it’s no wonder he caught people’s attention. 

If someone’s doing a quick search and doesn’t have time to check out every nominee, they’re definitely going to be drawn to the artist with 11 nominations over someone with just a couple (like Taylor Swift with Evermore, for instance). Plus, when you see that many nominations, it makes you think they’ve got a real shot at winning the big one, right?

Wow, that's a ton of votes that Batiste could potentially grab, and judging by Sunday night's results, it looks like he really did! This kind of thing isn't completely new in Grammy history. I mean, even in this century, we've seen some veteran artists who aren’t exactly pop stars, like Herbie Hancock with "River: The Joni Letters" in 2008, Alison Krauss with "Raising Sand" alongside Robert Plant in 2009, and even the soundtrack for "O Brother, Where Art Thou?" back in 2002, all take home the Album of the Year award. Lately, though, the winners have mostly been more contemporary mainstream artists. So, it seems like it took a mix of a really busy year and the Grammys expanding the Big Four categories from five nominations to eight, and now even ten this year, for something like this to happen. It’s kind of refreshing to see an unexpected winner from outside the top 40, right? Oh, and let’s not forget about industry connections. Even if you’re not the biggest pop sensation, it’s a big plus to be an artist that everyone knows and likes for one reason or another!

Hey there! So, this article is over a year old, but let’s chat about the Grammys this year. They’re really making some big changes by bumping up the number of nominees in the top categories from eight to ten. Can you believe it? That means someone could snag one of the biggest awards with just 11 percent of the votes! Crazy, right? The idea behind this is that “bigger” means “more inclusive,” but honestly, that logic doesn’t quite add up. Sure, making things more inclusive is super important for the Recording Academy, but Grammy night is all about prestige. To shift from being exclusive to more inclusive, you really need to invite fresh faces and let go of the usual suspects. But, let’s be real, the Academy isn’t great at shaking off old habits. And with that whole 11 percent situation, it seems like asking them to figure things out might be a bit much. The whole nominee expansion feels especially off when it comes to album of the year. It used to be the Academy’s top prize, but now it feels like it’s just for albums that were kind of designed to win. Still, these are the albums being celebrated as the best of the year. With the 64th Grammy Awards coming up this Sunday in Las Vegas, why not give them another listen? And speaking of fresh vibes, Taylor Swift is now diving into some wild sci-fi territory with her cozy new Pottery Barn aesthetic!

Alright, let’s chat about some of the big winners! First up, we’ve got the Best Rap Album category, where they’re looking for albums that have at least 51% new rap tracks. J. Cole’s "The Off-Season" and Nas’s "King's Disease II" were in the mix, but it was Tyler, The Creator’s "Call Me If You Get Lost" that snagged the win!

Moving on to Best Country Solo Performance, which highlights new vocal or instrumental solo country tracks, we had Luke Combs with "Forever After All," Mickey Guyton with "Remember Her Name," Jason Isbell’s "All I Do Is Drive," and Kacey Musgraves with "camera roll." But the top spot went to Chris Stapleton for "You Should Probably Leave." 

Next, in the Best Country Duo/Group Performance, we saw some cool collaborations. Jason Aldean and Carrie Underwood teamed up for "If I Didn't Love You," but it was Brothers Osborne’s "Younger Me" that took home the award. Dan + Shay’s "Glad You Exist," Ryan Hurd & Maren Morris’s "Chasing After You," and Elle King & Miranda Lambert’s "Drunk (And I Don’t Wanna Go Home)" were all contenders too!

Lastly, let’s talk about the Best Country Song, which is all about the songwriters. They’re looking for tracks that made waves this year. Jessie Jo Dillon, Maren Morris, Jimmy Robbins, and Laura Veltz teamed up for "Better Than We Found It," while Kacey Musgraves had her own with "camera roll." But the winner here was "Cold," written by Dave Cobb and J.T. Pretty exciting stuff!

So, here’s the scoop on the songwriting nominees: Powell II, Solána Rowe, and David Sprecher wrote "Leave The Door Open," which won the award, performed by Doja Cat featuring SZA. Then we have Brandon Anderson, Christopher Brody Brown, Dernst Emile II, and Bruno Mars behind "Montero (Call Me By Your Name)" by Lil Nas X, along with Denzel Baptiste, David Biral, Omer Fedi, Montero Hill, and Roy Lenzo. Justin Bieber's "Peaches," featuring Daniel Caesar and Giveon, had a whole crew of writers: Louis Bell, Giveon, Dezmann Evans, Bernard Harvey, Felisha "Fury" King, Matthew Sean Leon, Luis Manuel Martinez Jr., Aaron Simmonds, Ashton Simmonds, Andrew Wotman, and Keavan Yazdani. And then there’s "Right On Time" by Brandi Carlile, which was penned by her along with Dave Cobb, Phil Hanseroth, and Tim Hanseroth. 

Now, moving on to the Best New Artist category, which highlights an artist who really broke through and made waves this year. The nominees were Arooj Aftab, Jimmie Allen, Baby Keem, FINNEAS, Glass Animals, Japanese Breakfast, The Kid LAROI, Arlo Parks, and Olivia Rodrigo, who took home the win! And let’s not forget about the Best Pop Solo Performance category, which is all about those fresh vocal or instrumental pop tracks—just singles or tracks, you know?

So, here’s the scoop: the big event was supposed to go down at the Arena in Los Angeles, but on January 5, 2022, the Recording Academy hit the pause button on the ceremony because of health and safety worries about the COVID-19 Omicron variant. Then, on January 18, they rescheduled it for April 3, 2022, but moved the whole thing to the MGM Grand Garden Arena in Las Vegas because there were some scheduling clashes with the Crypto.com Arena. The nominations were revealed in a virtual livestream on November 23, 2021, hosted by Recording Academy CEO Harvey Mason Jr., along with some big names like Jon Batiste, Billie Eilish, Finneas O'Connell, H.E.R., BTS, Måneskin, Tayla Parx, Carly Pearce, comedian Nate Bargatze, chair Tammy Hurt, and Gayle King from CBS Mornings. Oh, and they brought back Trevor Noah to host the ceremony again! For 2022, the academy shook things up a bit by getting rid of the nomination review committees that used to pick the nominees. From now on, it’s all about the votes from the Recording Academy members.

You know, if 19-year-old Olivia Rodrigo scores a big win, it’s not just a win for those intense teenage feelings captured in her catchy tunes. It’s also a bit of a win for Billie Eilish, who took home the top four Grammy awards back in 2020, and Taylor Swift, a three-time album of the year champ. Plus, it could be a little embarrassing for the Recording Academy, which often puts artists in boxes — like the super talented young singer-songwriter type whose hits are always at the top. For Olivia, that big hit was “Drivers License,” a heartfelt ballad that really taps into that confusing teen phase and the excitement of what’s to come. If she walks away with the biggest award of the night, it should be for her creativity, not because the Grammy voters are stuck in their ways. And then there’s me, a 42-year-old White guy, reflecting on my thoughts when I first heard Lil Nas X’s debut album on September 17, 2021. I was like, “Why is this so different? What’s with those quirky music videos for pop songs that sound so mainstream?” I mean, does Lil Nas X want to fit in? I grew up feeling like I had to be normal, which just made me want to embrace the weird — from funky haircuts to offbeat music and all the attitude that comes with it.

Alright, let’s chat about some music awards! So, we’ve got the Best Remixed Recording, and it’s all about recognizing those remixers who really put their spin on tracks. Here’s the scoop: we’ve got some cool singles up for the award. There's “Back to Life” by Soul II Soul, remixed by Booker T in the Kings Of Soul Satta Dub version; then there’s “Born For Greatness” with a Cymek Remix from Spencer Bastian for Papa Roach. Don’t forget “Constant Craving” getting a Fashionably Late Remix by Tracy Young for K.D. Lang, and “Inside Out” remixed by 3SCAPE DRM from Zedd & Griff. Also on the list is “Met Him Last Night,” which got a remix from Dave Audé for Demi Lovato and Ariana Grande. And guess what? The winner is “Passenger,” remixed by Mike Shinoda from Deftones!

Now, moving on to the Best Immersive Audio Album, this category is all about those fantastic multichannel audio recordings that really take you there! To qualify, the recordings need to be available for sale or streaming in formats like DVD-Video, Blu-Ray, or even immersive downloads. They have to offer an original immersive mix with four or more channels, not just a rehash. Pretty neat, right?

Hey there! So, let’s dive into some of the nominees and winners from the recent awards. First up, we’ve got Double Dealin' Randy Brecker and Eric Marienthal with their track "The Garden," and Rachel Eckroth's "Tree Falls," which snagged the win. Then there's Taylor Eigsti at Blue Note Tokyo and the Steve Gadd Band with their album "Deep: The Baritone Sessions, Vol. 2," along with Mark Lettieri. 

Now, moving on to the Best Rock Performance category, which highlights new vocal or instrumental works in rock. The nominees included "Shot In The Dark" by AC/DC, "Know You Better (Live From Capitol Studio A)" by Black Pumas, "Nothing Compares 2 U" by Chris Cornell, and "Ohms" by Deftones. But the winner? That would be "Making A Fire" by Foo Fighters!

Next, let’s chat about the Best Metal Performance. This one’s all about new metal recordings, and the winner was "The Alien" by Dream Theater. Other nominees included "Amazonia" by Gojira, "Pushing The Tides" by Mastodon, and "The Triumph Of King Freak (A Crypt Of Preservation And Superstition)" by Rob Zombie.

And don’t forget about the Best Rock Song category, which celebrates songwriters for rock, hard rock, and metal. A song can qualify if it was released or gained popularity during the eligibility year. Just a heads up, this one’s all about singles or tracks only!

Alright, let’s chat about some of the Grammy categories! First up, we’ve got the Best Gospel Album. This one goes to albums that have at least 51% of their playing time packed with newly recorded gospel music—whether it’s traditional, contemporary, or even R&B. The nominees this time were “Changing Your Story” by Jekalyn Carr, “Royalty: Live At The Ryman” from Tasha Cobbs Leonard, “Jubilee: Juneteenth Edition” by Maverick City Music, and “Jonny X Mali: Live In LA” by Jonathan McReynolds & Mali Music. But the big winner? “Believe For It” by CeCe Winans!

Next, let’s dive into the Best Contemporary Christian Music Album. This award is for albums with at least 51% of their time dedicated to contemporary Christian tunes, including pop, rap/hip hop, Latin, or rock. The contenders were “No Stranger” from Natalie Grant, “Feels Like Home Vol. 2” by Israel & New Breed, “The Blessing (Live)” from Kari Jobe, and “Citizen Of Heaven (Live)” by Tauren Wells. And the winner here was “Old Church Basement” by Elevation Worship & Maverick City Music!

Now, moving on to the Best Roots Gospel Album, which celebrates albums with at least 51% of their time filled with traditional or roots gospel music—think country, Southern gospel, bluegrass, and Americana. The nominations included “Alone With My Faith” by Harry Connick, Jr., “That's Gospel, Brother” from Gaither Vocal Band, “Keeping On” by Ernie Haase & Signature Sound, and “Songs For The Times” by The Isaacs. The winner in this category was “My Savior” by Carrie Underwood!

And lastly, we have the Best Latin Pop Album, which is all about albums that feature at least 51% of new Latin pop recordings.

Hey there! Let’s talk about some of the nominees for this year’s awards. So, for Best Pop Solo Performance, we’ve got Justin Bieber with "Right On Time," Brandi Carlile’s "Happier Than Ever," Billie Eilish’s "Positions," and Ariana Grande’s "drivers license," which actually took home the win—congrats to Olivia Rodrigo! 

Moving on to Best Pop Duo/Group Performance, it's all about those collaborations. We’ve got "I Get A Kick Out Of You" by Tony Bennett and Lady Gaga, "Lonely" from Justin Bieber and benny blanco, BTS with "Butter," and Coldplay’s "Higher Power." But the winner here was "Kiss Me More" by Doja Cat featuring SZA. 

Now, let’s chat about Best Traditional Pop Vocal Album. This category is for albums that have at least 51% of new traditional pop recordings. The winner was "Love For Sale" by Tony Bennett and Lady Gaga, while we also saw albums like "Til We Meet Again (Live)" from Norah Jones and "A Tori Kelly Christmas" from Tori Kelly.

For Best Pop Vocal Album, the nominees include Justin Bieber’s "Justice (Triple Chucks Deluxe)," Doja Cat’s "Planet Her (Deluxe)," Billie Eilish’s "Happier Than Ever," and Ariana Grande’s "Positions." But Olivia Rodrigo’s "Sour" snagged the win!

Lastly, for Best Dance/Electronic Recording, we’ve got performances from solo artists, duos, and groups, whether vocal or instrumental. Just Singles or tracks—lots to look forward to!

So, here’s the scoop: Bashar Jackson, Sean Jacob, Nima Jahanbin, Paimon Jahanbin, Syleena Johnson, Dominique Armani Jones, Eli Klughammer, Chinsea Lee, Mike Lévy, Evan Mast, Mark Mbogo, Miles McCollum, Josh Mease, Scott Medcudi, Brian Miller, Rodrick Wayne Moore Jr., Michael Mulé, Mark Myrie, Charles M. Njapa, Nasir Pemberton, Carlos St. John Phillips, Jason Phillips, Khalil Abdul Rahman, Laraya Ashlee Robinson, Christopher Ruelas, David Ruoff, Maxie Lee Ryles III, Matthew Samuels, Daniel Seeff, Eric Sloan Jr., Sean Solymar, Ronald O’Neill Spence Jr., David Styles, Michael Suski, Aqeel Tate, Abel Makkonen Tesfaye, Caleb Zackery Toliver, Bastian Völkel, Brian Hugh Warner, Jacques Webster II, Kanye West, Orlando Wilder, Jeffery Williams, and Mark Williams are all the talented songwriters behind this. Plus, we’ve got Irko handling the mastering. Now, about the Song of the Year Award: a song can be in the running if it was first released or really took off during the Eligibility Year. Just a heads-up, we’re talking about singles or tracks only!

You know, it's pretty cool to see all the amazing elements of country music come together — from those catchy pop hooks to the gritty red-dirt outlaw vibes. We've got some rising stars, leading ladies, and country legends really shining right now. Take Miranda Lambert, for example. She gave a heartfelt performance with her song "In His Arms," which she co-wrote while soaking in the vibes of the windswept west Texas highlands. Then there's Zach Bryan, a viral newcomer who tapped into that same organic feel with "Something in the Orange," just him and his acoustic guitar on the Oklahoma side of the Red River. And we can’t forget about Maren Morris and Kelsea Ballerini, both of whom burst onto the scene in the 2010s and are still making waves. Morris brought her Texas grit to the playful "Circles Around This Town," while Ballerini mixed in some catchy pop with her upbeat track "HEARTFIRST." And of course, we've got the legendary Willie Nelson in the mix, paying tribute to his late friend Billy Joe Shaver with a beautiful cover of "Live Forever." How fitting for the 89-year-old icon, who's been in the game for nearly eight decades! With the 2023 GRAMMYs just around the corner on February 5, it’s a great time to dive into this year's nominees for Best Country Solo Performance.
Hey, let’s talk about the Best Latin Rock or Alternative Album category! The excitement just keeps rolling, and this year, we have some great nominees, including Cimafunk's...

So, guess what? Silk Sonic totally took home both Record of the Year and Song of the Year for "Leave the Door Open"! And on top of that, Olivia Rodrigo snagged the Best New Artist award. By the way, the ceremony was originally set to...

"Hey there! Check out the live roll call of the winners from the 64th annual Grammys, which we'll keep updating. Oh, and guess what? The big winner took home five Grammy Awards this year, including Album of the Year!"
So, on March 31, 2022, it’s no shocker that “Folklore” picked up album of the year at last year's Grammys. If “Evermore” does the same this year, it’s really gonna make 2022 feel…

"Hey, did you catch Harry Styles' acceptance speech for Album of the Year for 'Harry's House' at the 2023 Grammys on February 5th? It was pretty memorable! And just a little background on the Recording Academy and the Grammys..."

query: When is the final of the 2022 FIFA World Cup?
positive:
So, you know how France was the defending champion after they took down Croatia 4-2 in the 2018 FIFA World Cup Final? Well, the next World Cup was set to happen from November 20 to December 18 in Qatar, which was pretty special since it was the first time the tournament was being held in the Arab world. And guess what? Fans didn’t have to bother with most COVID-19 restrictions like social distancing or wearing masks. 

Now, here’s something interesting: instead of the usual June and July dates, the 2022 World Cup was pushed to November and December because Qatar can get super hot and humid in the summer. This change meant that the World Cup fell right in the middle of the season for a lot of domestic leagues that kick off in late July or August. To make it work, those major European leagues had to figure out some extended breaks in their schedules. They even planned their group matches before the World Cup so they wouldn’t have to deal with scheduling conflicts the following year. FIFA confirmed the match schedule back in July 2020, and the group stage was all set to kick off on November 21 with four matches happening every day!

So, on February 24, 2015, the FIFA Task Force suggested that the World Cup be held from late November to late December 2022. The idea was to steer clear of the scorching summer heat from May to September and avoid overlapping with the Winter Olympics in February, the Winter Paralympics in March, and Ramadan in April. But, moving the tournament to November stirred up quite a bit of controversy since it would mess with the regular season schedules of domestic leagues worldwide. People pointed out that it would clash with the Christmas season, which could really shake things up, and there were worries about how short the tournament was going to be. FIFA executive committee member Theo Zwanziger even called giving the 2022 World Cup to Qatar a "blatant mistake." Frank Lowy, who’s the chairman of Football Federation Australia, mentioned that if the World Cup got pushed to November and disrupted the A-League schedule, they’d be looking for compensation from FIFA. And Richard Scudamore, the Premier League chief executive, said they might even think about taking legal action against FIFA because such a change would interfere with their popular Christmas and New Year games. Then on March 19, 2015, FIFA confirmed that the final would indeed take place on December 18.

So, the 2022 FIFA World Cup final was the big showdown of the tournament, which was the 22nd time this competition for men's national football teams took place. It all went down at Lusail Stadium in Lusail, Qatar, on December 18, 2022, which also happened to be Qatar's National Day. The match featured Argentina going up against the defending champions, France. Can you believe it? There were about 88,966 fans in the stands, and a whopping 1.5 billion people tuned in on TV, making it one of the most-watched sports events ever! 

The tournament had Qatar as the host and 31 other teams that made it through the qualification rounds set up by the six FIFA confederations. These 32 teams battled it out in a group stage, and from there, 16 teams moved on to the knockout rounds. Argentina kicked things off by finishing first in Group C. They had a bit of a rough patch losing to Saudi Arabia 2–1, but then bounced back with wins against Mexico and Poland, both 2–0. After that, they took down Australia in the round of 16, edged past the Netherlands in the quarter-finals in a nail-biting penalty shootout, and finally beat Croatia in the semi-finals. 

On the other side, France topped Group D with two wins and one loss (they had a 4–1 victory over Australia, a 2–1 win against Denmark, and a 1–0 loss to Tunisia). They powered through to defeat Poland in the round of 16, overcame England in the quarter-finals, and took down Morocco in the semi-finals. What a journey for both teams!

You can catch all the live action and full replays of the FIFA World Cup 2022™ on FOXSports.com and the FOX Sports App. If you miss a game, don’t worry—full replays will also be on Tubi. Since it’s super hot in Qatar, they decided to shift the tournament from its usual summer spot. The big finale is happening at the impressive 80,000-seat Lusail Iconic Stadium on December 18, 2022—just a week before Christmas! By the way, check out all the kits that have been released for Qatar 2022 so far!

So, the 2022 FIFA World Cup was the 22nd time this big event happened, and it’s basically the world championship for national football teams organized by FIFA. It went down in Qatar from November 20 to December 18, 2022, after Qatar got the hosting gig back in 2010. This was a pretty special World Cup since it was the first one held in the Arab world and the Muslim world, and the second one ever to be fully hosted in Asia—after the 2002 tournament in South Korea and Japan. 

Oh, and this was also the last World Cup with 32 teams; starting in 2026, we’ll see that number jump to 48! To dodge Qatar’s extreme heat, they decided to hold the event in November and December. It lasted for 29 days, packed with 64 matches played in eight venues across five cities. Qatar, being the host, automatically got a spot in the tournament for their first-ever World Cup, alongside 31 other teams that qualified. 

In the end, Argentina took home the trophy after a nail-biting final against France, winning 4–2 on penalties after a 3–3 draw in extra time. This was Argentina's third title, marking their first win since 1986, and they became the first nation outside of Europe to snag the trophy since 2002.

So, later on, they decided to shuffle the schedule a bit and moved the Qatar vs Ecuador game to November 20th. Qatar really pushed FIFA to let them kick off the tournament. The final match took place on December 18, 2022, which was also National Day, at Lusail Stadium. As for the games in each group, FIFA laid out the venues and kick-off times on April 1, 2022, right after the draw. Then on August 11, it was announced that Qatar vs Ecuador would now be the opening match, shifted a day earlier, while Senegal vs Netherlands got the new spot that opened up. Back in April 2022, FIFA also revealed the prize money for all the teams involved. Each team that qualified got $1.5 million upfront for preparations, and they were guaranteed at least $9 million in prize money. The total prize pool for this edition was a hefty $440 million, which is $40 million more than the last tournament. Oh, and this World Cup introduced some new rules for substitutions, allowing teams to make up to five changes during regular time and one extra change in overtime. Plus, it was the first World Cup to allow concussion substitutions, so each team could use one special substitute if a concussion was suspected during a match.

Hey everyone! Can you believe it? The biggest sporting event in the world is back, and it’s happening every four years! That’s right, the FIFA World Cup QATAR 2022 kicked off last month, and the final is just around the corner. Argentina got off to a great start with early goals from Lionel Messi and Ángel Di María, putting them ahead 2-0. 

France struggled a bit in the first half and didn’t even manage a shot until after the 70th minute, but things changed when they made some substitutions around the 71st minute. A few minutes later, Randal Kolo Muani got fouled in the box by Nicolás Otamendi, and France was awarded a penalty. Kylian Mbappé stepped up and scored that penalty, and just two minutes later, he found the back of the net again to level the score at 2-2. 

With the match tied, they headed into extra time. Messi scored again in the 108th minute, putting Argentina back in the lead. But then, in the 115th minute, Mbappé was awarded another penalty after his shot hit Gonzalo Montiel's arm. So, Harry Kane managed to score a penalty for England, but sadly, it wasn’t enough to take down France. They won 2–1, thanks to goals from Aurélien Tchouaméni and Olivier Giroud, plus Kane's missed penalty later on. This win sent France to their second World Cup semi-final in a row, making them the first defending champions to make it this far since Brazil did it back in 1998. The semi-finals took place on December 13 and 14. In one of those matches, Messi scored a penalty and Julián Álvarez added two more, leading Argentina to a solid 3–0 win over Croatia. That was their biggest win in the 2022 tournament and it marked their sixth final appearance, the first since 2014. Over in the other semi, Théo Hernandez put France ahead against Morocco just five minutes in. Randal Kolo Muani sealed the deal with a goal in the 78th minute, wrapping up a 2–0 victory for France and sending them to their second straight final. Then, there was the third place play-off on December 17. Croatia and Morocco had faced off earlier in the group stage, ending in a 0–0 draw. This time, Joško Gvardiol scored first for Croatia, but Achraf Dari quickly equalized. In the end, Mislav Oršić netted the winner for Croatia, and they took the match 2–1.

So, Antoine Griezmann got things rolling by scoring a penalty, but then Argentina took the lead with goals from Ángel Di María and Gabriel Mercado. After that, France really turned things around, scoring three goals in a row—first with a stunning volley from Benjamin Pavard, which ended up being voted the goal of the tournament, and then Kylian Mbappé found the net twice. Just when it looked like Argentina was down for the count, Sergio Agüero managed to score in stoppage time, bringing the score to 4-3, but they couldn't quite get that equalizer to push the match into extra time. 

On another note, the match ball for the semi-finals, third place match, and final of the 2022 FIFA World Cup was revealed on December 11, 2022. It’s a twist on the Adidas Al Rihla that they used throughout the tournament and is called the Adidas Al-Hilm, which means "The Dream" in Arabic—kind of fitting since every nation dreams of lifting that World Cup trophy. While the ball's technical features are the same, the colors are different from the Al-Rihla balls we saw in the group stages and earlier knockout games. This one sports a Gold Metallic, maroon, Collegiate Burgundy, and red design, nodding to the colors of Qatar, the host nation, and the golden hues of the Lusail Stadium, where the final took place, along with the FIFA World Cup Trophy itself.

Did you know that this was the fifth special ball made for the FIFA World Cup finals? Before this one, there were the Teamgeist Berlin, Jo'bulani, Brazuca Final Rio, and Telstar Mechta. The final match took place at Lusail Stadium in Lusail, Qatar, which is about 15 kilometers (or 9.3 miles) north of downtown Doha. They had this stadium in mind right from the start when Qatar was bidding for the World Cup, and they officially confirmed it as the final venue on July 15, 2020. 

Lusail Stadium was also set to host nine other World Cup matches, including six group stage games and three knockout rounds. Owned by the Qatar Football Association, it was built specifically for Qatar's World Cup bid. The design came from a collaboration between British firms Foster and Partners and Populous, with help from MANICA Architecture. Oh, and it’s pretty cool that the stadium uses solar power for cooling and is said to have a carbon zero footprint! 

Construction kicked off in April 2017 and was supposed to wrap up in 2020, but there were some delays, and it actually got finished in November 2021. The stadium finally held its first match, the Lusail Super Cup, on September 9, 2022, which was later than everyone had hoped for.

So, you know how COVID-19 affected a lot of things? Well, Vanuatu and Cook Islands had to back out because of travel restrictions. Out of the 32 teams that qualified for the 2022 FIFA World Cup, 24 of them had actually played in the last tournament back in 2018. And guess what? Qatar was the only team making their debut this time around, which is pretty special since they’re the first hosts to do that since Italy back in 1934. That means the 2022 World Cup was unique because none of the teams that got in through qualifying were first-timers. 

On a brighter note, the Netherlands, Ecuador, Ghana, Cameroon, and the United States all made a comeback after missing out in 2018. Canada is also back after a whopping 36 years—last time they played was in 1986! And then there’s Wales, making their first showing in 64 years, which is the longest gap for any team, since they last played in 1958. 

Oh, and it’s worth mentioning that Italy, the four-time champions and reigning European champs, didn’t qualify for a second World Cup in a row for the first time ever. They lost in the qualification play-off semi-finals, which is a big deal. They were the only former champions and the highest-ranked team in FIFA's Men's World Rankings that didn't make the cut.

Now, let’s talk about the bidding for the 2018 and 2022 FIFA World Cups. It kicked off back in January 2009, and national associations had until February 2, 2009, to show their interest. Initially, there were 11 bids for the 2018 World Cup, but then Mexico decided to pull out. Indonesia’s bid was also a no-go; FIFA rejected it in February 2010 because the Indonesian Football Association didn’t submit a letter from their government to back the bid.

Once UEFA was confirmed to host the 2018 event, they were out of the running for 2022. That left us with five contenders for the 2022 World Cup: Australia, Japan, Qatar, South Korea, and the United States. So, let’s break down the matches from December! On Saturday, December 3rd, the Netherlands took on the USA and won 3-1, while Argentina edged out Australia with a score of 2-1. Then, on Sunday, December 4th, France beat Poland 3-1, and England had a solid game against Senegal, winning 3-0.

And then, on Saturday, December 3, the Netherlands came out on top against 349 with a score of 3-0. What a series of matches!

So, here’s how the draw worked for the tournament. Pot 1 had host Qatar, who automatically got the A1 spot, along with the seven best teams. Then, in Pot 2, we had the next eight best teams, followed by another eight in Pot 3. Pot 4 was reserved for the five lowest-ranked teams and placeholders for the winners of two inter-confederation playoffs and the UEFA Path A playoff. Now, there was a rule that teams from the same confederation couldn’t be in the same group, except for the UEFA teams, where there could be one or two per group. This rule also applied to the placeholder teams, depending on the confederation of the potential playoff winners. The draw kicked off with Pot 1 and wrapped up with Pot 4, and each team was placed into the first available group in alphabetical order. The position for each team within their group was drawn afterward, with the Pot 1 teams automatically filling position 1 in each group. By the way, FIFA also announced the list of officials in May 2022, which included 36 referees, 69 assistant referees, and 24 video assistant referees for the tournament. query: How many vehicles did Tesla deliver in the first quarter of 2022?
positive: Hey there! So, Tesla just shared their numbers for the first quarter of 2022, and here's the scoop. They delivered a total of 310,048 electric vehicles and produced 305,407. To give you some context, during the same time last year, they delivered 184,800 and made 180,338 cars. Most of the deliveries this time around—about 95%, or 295,324—were from their Model 3 and Model Y vehicles. They did run into some hiccups though, producing 4,641 fewer cars than they delivered because of ongoing supply chain issues and some factory shutdowns. Analysts were actually expecting them to hit around 317,000 vehicle deliveries for those first three months of the year, based on estimates from FactSet as of March 31.

Hey there! So, Tesla's first quarter results for 2022 are pretty impressive. They hit another record for deliveries, and their production was almost at an all-time high too. Given how tough the global economy and the car industry are right now, that’s really something! In this post, we’re going to dig into the numbers and charts to see how they’re doing and what we might expect in the near future. 

Alright, let’s break it down. Tesla ramped up its electric car production in Q1 by about 69% compared to last year, reaching 305,407 vehicles—just a hair short of the record they set in Q4 2021. Most of those were Model 3s and Model Ys, with over 291,000 units. And here’s something else to be excited about: the refreshed Model S and Model X saw a nice bump in production too, compared to the last quarter. 

When it comes to sales, they delivered a record-breaking 310,048 vehicles this quarter, which is a 68% increase from the previous year! The Model 3 and Model Y really shined, setting their own all-time records, while the Model S and X are getting closer to the levels we saw in earlier years. 

Overall, in just the last four quarters, Tesla has produced and delivered more than a million electric cars. That’s pretty impressive, right?

So, Tesla just released its vehicle production and delivery numbers for the second quarter of 2022, and here’s the scoop: their delivery figures came in a bit lower than what analysts were hoping for. According to some estimates from FactSet-owned Street Account, they were looking for around 256,520 vehicles to be delivered this quarter, but you know, with all those Covid restrictions, supply chain issues, and shortages of semiconductor chips and other parts, it’s been a tough time. Last year, during the same quarter, Tesla managed to deliver 201,250 vehicles, marking the first time they crossed the 200,000 mark in a three-month stretch. And just to give you a quick comparison, in the first quarter of 2022, they delivered a whopping 310,048 vehicles. So, today’s delivery numbers show a sales growth of 26%.

"Hey, did you hear that in the first quarter of 2022, Tesla delivered a whopping 310,048 electric vehicles? That’s pretty impressive! Deliveries are actually the best way to gauge how many cars they sold."
negative: So, here’s the scoop on Tesla's first-quarter deliveries: they came in at 422,875 vehicles, which is a bit shy of what analysts were expecting—around 430,008, according to Refinitiv data from seven analysts. FactSet estimated the number would be around 432,000, and that’s what Wall Street was hoping for, as reported by the Wall Street Journal and CNBC. While Tesla didn’t quite meet those Refinitiv and FactSet figures, other estimates show they actually exceeded some Wall Street expectations. Bloomberg analysts thought the number would be closer to 421,164, and Tesla even mentioned that a group of over 20 analysts predicted around 421,500 vehicles delivered—though Reuters couldn’t confirm that number. It seems like there’s a bit of a mix-up in those consensus figures, as Munster pointed out. On the bright side, Tesla did deliver 6% more of its popular Model 3 and Model Y cars compared to the last quarter, but they saw a big drop of 38% in deliveries for their pricier Model X and Model S. Oh, and just to add, they actually produced more cars than they delivered, cranking out 440,808 vehicles in those first three months of the year!

"Wow, over 2.6 million Tesla cars have been delivered so far! query: Who acquired Twitter?
positive: Hey there! So, let’s catch up on some big news. On April 25, 2022, Twitter announced that it’s going to be acquired by a company fully owned by Elon Musk, and the deal is worth about $44 billion. Then, on May 25, Musk changed his game plan, deciding not to use margin loans against Tesla stock for the deal, and instead pledged an extra $6.25 billion in equity financing. Oh, and Dorsey left Twitter's board on the same day. Meanwhile, a Twitter investor named William Heresniak slapped Musk with a class-action lawsuit, claiming he broke California corporate laws by messing with the market. The lawsuit also pointed out that Musk wasn't allowed to put the deal on hold and that his misleading comments had hurt Twitter's stock prices. Fast forward to June 3, and the acquisition got the green light from U.S. antitrust review. Just three days later, Musk's attorney shot an email to Twitter, warning that Musk might want to back out of the agreement because Twitter wouldn't share user data with him.

So, Agrawal, Segal, and Gadde were supposed to get some pretty hefty "golden parachute" payouts—$38.7 million, $25.4 million, and $12.5 million, respectively. But guess what? The New York Times said that Musk probably wouldn't be paying up since the execs were let go for cause. The Financial Times mentioned that Musk thought the company had been mismanaged, and now those executives are looking into their legal options because of it. Meanwhile, Dorsey still holds onto his $1 billion stake in the company, and a few other executives left Twitter in the days that followed. Musk took over as CEO, combined the company with X Holdings, and even disbanded Twitter's board of directors. With this merger, Twitter wasn’t its own independent entity anymore; they created X Corp. in March 2023 to take over. Musk likes to call himself the "Chief Twit" now. Plus, they set up a "war room" at Twitter where Musk met with Spiro, Sacks, and others to figure out what to do next. According to The New York Times, the main goals for this group were to cut down Twitter's workforce and revamp the mobile app.

So, during a video call with the banks that helped fund his buyout, Musk assured them he’d wrap things up by the deadline. On October 26, he even visited Twitter's headquarters, posting a fun video of himself carrying a kitchen sink in the lobby and changing his Twitter bio to "Chief Twit." He told Twitter employees that while some layoffs were probably going to happen, he didn’t plan to go about it the way the Post had suggested. The following day, he wrote an open letter to advertisers saying that Twitter wouldn’t turn into a “free-for-all hellscape.” He emphasized that his reasons for buying Twitter weren’t just about making money; he wanted to create “a common digital town square.” Then, he asked Tesla engineers to meet up with Twitter's product managers to check out the platform's code, which was on hold until November 1. Later, on October 27, Musk and Twitter finalized the deal, and he tweeted, “the bird is freed.” Just like that, he became Twitter's new owner and immediately fired Agrawal, CFO Ned Segal, Gadde, and general counsel Sean Edgett, with security escorting them out of the building.

So, here’s the scoop: At the beginning of this year, Elon Musk decided to stake a claim in Twitter, and then in April, he made this jaw-dropping $44 billion offer. Right from the get-go, that price seemed a bit steep. He said he wanted to buy the platform to create a "common digital town square" for everyone, plus he had plans to tackle spam accounts and keep it a space for free speech. But then, come summer, he had a change of heart. He started raising concerns that there were way more fake accounts on Twitter than the company was admitting. 

And then there’s the whole drama with Twitter’s executives taking legal action to hold him to that big offer. Even though Elon recently acknowledged that he and the other investors were "overpaying for Twitter right now," it seems like the deal is finally happening. But people are still buzzing about what he really plans to do with the platform. Just the other day, he switched his Twitter profile to "Chief Twit," and some folks think he might actually take charge himself. Oh, and he even posted a fun video of himself walking into Twitter’s headquarters in San Francisco with a sink, captioning it: "let that sink in!" As of now, Twitter hasn’t said anything about the new management team.

So, here’s the scoop: he started asking a different question - how many real accounts are actually on Twitter? This billionaire, who’s been labeled the richest person in the world by Forbes and Bloomberg with a jaw-dropping net worth of around $250 billion (£216 billion), has been grumbling about the number of bots on the platform for years. Once his offer was accepted, he kept pushing Twitter for data on how many real users were out there. Twitter’s execs claimed that less than 5% of daily active users, based on some random account checks, were bots. This seemed to really tick him off. After a lengthy Twitter thread from Mr. Agrawal explaining how they came up with that number, Mr. Musk replied with a poop emoji. Yeah, things were definitely going south. Not surprisingly, on July 8, he announced he wanted to back out of the deal. Was he just trying to negotiate a better price, or was he really done? Who knows? Twitter wasn’t having any of it. They argued that his agreement to buy the company was legally binding and there was no way to backtrack now. With pricey lawyers on both sides, they set a court date in Delaware for October 17 to figure out if Mr. Musk would be forced to go through with the purchase. In the court documents, Twitter insisted they had given him plenty of info about their real user count.

You know, I’m not really sure how buying Twitter would help him get his message out better or tell a story that benefits Tesla and his financial interests. But then again, it’s not completely clear that it wouldn’t either. He’s a pretty sharp guy. One angle to consider is that Twitter doesn’t rake in as much cash compared to Facebook and other social media platforms. It’s not exactly a giant when it comes to market value. He might think, “Hey, I really value this direct line to the public.” Owning that direct access—something that brings a lot of value to both him and Tesla—has to mean something, right? Whether it boosts Tesla’s value or helps monetize the buzz it creates for celebrities, athletes, and even Donald Trump, there’s potential there. Just think about it: Trump’s tweets back when he was on Twitter could lead to billions or even trillions in market shifts, and Twitter didn’t really profit much from that. It’s not clear how the company would make money, but if you’re in control of something that can create that level of value, you’d think a smart person could figure out how to cash in on it.

So, Musk found himself needing an extra $22.4 billion to cover the equity financing part of his deal. But then, the co-backers, Apollo Global Management and Sixth Street Partners, decided to back out. You might remember that photo of Musk’s Twitter account on a smartphone in front of the Twitter logo from April 2022. Anyway, at 51, Musk is still the richest guy in the world with a staggering net worth of $222 billion, though a big chunk of that is tied up in Tesla and SpaceX. After selling off some Tesla shares last November, December, and again in April and August, he had about $20 billion in cash. To fully finance the deal, he needed to raise another $2 billion to $3 billion. It’s a bit of a mystery how he managed to fill that $3 billion gap. Wedbush analyst Daniel Ives suggested it could be outside capital since there were no Form 4s filed this week, and it’s likely someone who’s already on board with the deal. People were thinking he might sell more Tesla shares during the nine days between the electric car company’s results on October 19 and the October 28 deadline to wrap things up.

So, Jeff Bezos, the guy who started Amazon, was wondering if Tesla's interest in China might give the Chinese government some power over Twitter through Musk. He figured it "probably not." Then you've got Bill Gates, the Microsoft co-founder, asking if Musk would let public health misinformation, like vaccine myths, run rampant on the platform. Jimmy Wales, who helped create Wikipedia, thought Twitter might either do really well or crash and burn in the next five years under Musk's leadership. On the flip side, Bitcoin investor Roger Ver and Coinbase CEO Brian Armstrong were pretty excited about the buyout, seeing it as a chance to reduce what they thought was censorship on Twitter. Sundar Pichai, the CEO of Google and Alphabet, expressed hope that Twitter would get better over time because it really matters to society. Meanwhile, Mark Zuckerberg from Facebook and Meta seemed a bit confused and uncertain about Musk's plans. Edgett told employees to keep their opinions on the supposed cancellation to themselves, but a few Twitter folks couldn't resist sharing some funny takes on the whole thing. After Musk's announcement, Tesla's shares jumped by 2.11 percent. Trump didn’t hold back, criticizing Musk and calling the acquisition "rotten," which sparked a long feud between them. Aside from Trump, most conservatives were on Musk's side, with Steve Bannon, the former White House strategist, going after Twitter for supposedly lying about its spambot accounts, and Charlie Kirk from Turning Point USA speculating that Musk might have been trying to "expose" Twitter all along.
So, on April 14, Musk jumped in with an unsolicited offer to buy Twitter for a whopping $43 billion, which breaks down to $54.20 a share, and he wants to take it private. Even though...
"Hey, so back on October 27, 2022, after what felt like ages of back and forth, legal battles, and a lot of drama, Elon Musk finally became the owner of Twitter."

So, back on October 28, 2022, he came in with a whopping $44 billion offer for Twitter, but it was pretty much a take it or leave it deal. At first, Twitter's board wasn't having it and even cooked up a "poison pill" strategy to fend him off...

On October 28, 2022, Elon Musk officially took the reins at Twitter, letting go of its CEO and a few other high-ranking folks. You know, there’s been a lot of chatter about why he decided to buy Twitter and what his plans are...
